payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: can both work together?

payload.message: can both work together?

Yes, but not by тАЬtalkingтАЭ directly.

They work together through shared repo artifacts.

**The actual coordination layer**
- `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- shared types/schema file
- `validation-instructions.md`
- optional `status/` files
- normal git commits/checkpoints

So the flow is:

1. One tool creates the contract pack.
2. Both tools read the same contract.
3. Builders work in separate scopes.
4. Validator reads the same contract and checks compliance.
5. Integration happens after validation.

**Best split**
- Claude: contract generation, builder orchestration, maybe integration
- Codex: validator, adversarial review, or one independent builder scope

That is the cleanest setup because:
- Claude skill now handles contract-first scaffolding well
- Codex skill is good at producing repo-ready contract plans too
- using one as builder and one as validator gives you uncorrelated failure modes

**What not to do**
- Do not let both write the same directory
- Do not let both edit shared contract files
- Do not let both тАЬownтАЭ integration
- Do not rely on memory or chat history between them

**When it works well**
It works well if:
- ownership map is clean
- shared types are frozen
- validator is report-only
- integration is a separate final step

If those are true, yes, both can absolutely work together.

If you want, I can map the exact Claude-role / Codex-role split IтАЩd use for your first real run.

content.text: so for contracts i can set it to half codex half claude? and let codex build codex and claude build claude??\

payload.message: so for contracts i can set it to half codex half claude? and let codex build codex and claude build claude??\

No. Do not split the contract 50/50.

That is the one thing that should have a single owner.

**Good split**
- One tool drafts the contract
- The other tool reviews or red-teams the contract
- You approve the final version
- Then both can build against it in separate scopes

**Bad split**
- Claude writes half of `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- Codex writes the other half
- Both invent types, routes, names, and ownership rules independently

That creates drift immediately.

The right pattern is:

1. Claude or Codex creates the full contract pack.
2. The other tool audits it and points out gaps.
3. You lock the final contract.
4. Then split implementation:
   - Claude builds modules A and B
   - Codex builds modules C and D
5. Validator checks all modules against the same frozen contract.
6. One integration owner wires the final result.

So:
- `contract`: single writer, second reviewer
- `implementation`: many writers, disjoint scopes
- `validation`: separate from builders
- `integration`: single owner

If you really want both involved in contract creation, the safe version is:
- Claude writes contract draft 1
- Codex writes review comments or a competing draft
- you merge to one final contract
- after that, nobody edits it casually

**Short rule**
DonтАЩt split the spec.
Split the work built from the spec.

A strong setup would be:
- Claude: contract architect + builder for some modules
- Codex: contract reviewer + builder for different modules
- Codex or Claude fresh session: validator
- one tool only: integration

If you want, I can sketch the exact ownership map for a 4-module feature so you can see what this looks like in practice.

- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Examples

- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.

content.text: i meant opus will write the contracts and delegate half to opus and half to codex but how does codex build?

payload.message: i meant opus will write the contracts and delegate half to opus and half to codex but how does codex build?

Opus can write the contracts, yes. But Opus does not directly delegate into Codex.

You do.

The actual flow is:

1. Opus writes the contract pack
   - `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
   - shared types/schema
   - `validation-instructions.md`
   - ownership map

2. You lock that contract
   - quick review
   - commit/checkpoint if needed

3. You start Codex separately
   - same repo, or better, separate branch/worktree
   - give Codex a builder prompt against the contract

Codex builds by reading the repo artifacts, not by тАЬhearingтАЭ Opus.

Use a prompt like:

```text
Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md and src/types/shared.ts.
You own only src/payments/**.
Implement the payments module exactly to contract.
Do not edit shared contract files.
Do not write outside your scope.
If the contract is insufficient, stop and report the gap instead of inventing a new interface.
```

So the real split is:

- Opus: contract architect, maybe Builder A/B
- Codex: Builder C/D on different scopes
- Validator: fresh session, report-only
- Integrator: one owner only

The important part is that Codex only builds from:
- the frozen contract
- the shared types
- its assigned write scope

Not from Opus conversation history.

Best practice:
- separate scopes
- separate branches or worktrees
- one integration owner
- no casual edits to contract after builders start

So yes, this works, but the wording should be:

- Opus writes the contract
- you dispatch Opus builders for half
- you dispatch Codex builders for half
- both build against the same frozen contract

Not тАЬOpus delegates half to CodexтАЭ directly.

If you want, I can write the exact builder prompt template for Codex and the matching one for Opus.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

README
Mechanize Take-Home: Collaborative Whiteboard
Thank you for taking the time to interview with Mechanize! This repo contains your assignment for the final phase of our interview process, an open-ended take-home assignment.

The take-home begins with a solo work block of three hours in which you can complete the task as described below.

There is a 20-minute break after the solo block. During this time, our team will review your submission. If we'd like to move forward based on your progress, we'll talk to you on the follow-up call. If not, we'll cancel the follow-up calendar invite and send you an email cancelling the call.

Getting started
Clone this repo (do not fork it):
git clone <this-repo-url>
cd <this-repo-name>
See SETUP.md for how to run the scaffold.
Do all of your work on the main branch. Commit early and often.
Push your commits directly to main тАФ do not open a pull request.
git push origin main
Your final commit must be pushed no later than three hours after your start time. Anything pushed after the deadline will not be considered.
What you're building
A real-time collaborative whiteboard. Multiple users, same canvas, editing together.

Deployment context
This whiteboard is for synchronous design reviews, consisting of a small group (typically 2-5 people) on a call together, using the canvas as a shared scratchpad while they talk through a design.

Each user has an account and can create canvases or be invited to others'.
It's common for several people to be editing at once -- often the same objects -- as they try alternatives or work through a disagreement in real time.
A typical session runs 30-90 minutes.
Teams sometimes return to a canvas in a later session to recall what was decided.
Required features
User accounts (signup, login, logout)
Create a canvas, open an existing canvas
Invite other users to a canvas (by username or email -- a simple mechanism is fine)
Shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, text
Select, move, resize, delete, change color
Undo / redo
See other users' cursors
Canvas state persists across sessions
Out of scope
Don't spend time on these feature, as we won't evaluate them:

Collaborative text editing within text elements (treating a text update as a single operation is fine -- you don't need to handle two users typing in the same text box simultaneously)
Granular permissions (viewer/editor/owner roles, share-with-link, etc.)
Rich text editing, images, or file uploads
Canvas zoom, pan, or infinite canvas
Export (PNG, SVG, PDF)
Version history or named snapshots
Mobile or touch support
Provided scaffold
This repository contains a minimal scaffold -- frontend, backend, and database wired together, but no whiteboard logic. You are free to restructure anything in the scaffold, but you are expected to stay within the provided stack; don't swap out the framework or database.

How we evaluate
We evaluate whether your submission has a functional implementation of all required features above and is fit for the deployment context. Correctness and robustness for this use case and set of features matters more to us than other features beyond the required list, such as those listed as out of scope..

Use of AI
Use whatever AI tools you like. We recommend that you use frontier AI coding models. We are not aware of a candidate who has performed exceptionally on this take-home without using AI. However, note that you will be expected to fully understand and defend your implementation during the call after the solo block.

About
No description, website, or topics provided.
Resources
 Readme
 Activity
 Custom properties
Stars
 0 stars
Watchers
 0 watching
Forks
 0 forks
Releases
No releases published
Create a new release

can we use the skill on this?

Yes, but use it carefully.

This repo is a good fit for the skill because the hard part is not scaffolding, itтАЩs freezing the shared contract for:
- shape schema
- whiteboard operation model
- websocket message types
- cursor/presence events
- undo/redo semantics
- persistence boundaries
- auth/invite surface

That is exactly what the skill is for.

**Where it helps most**
Use the skill at the start to produce a contract pack, not to immediately unleash a big write swarm.

For this take-home, the riskiest drift is:
- client and server disagreeing on shape/event payloads
- undo/redo semantics not matching persistence
- presence/cursor events being modeled separately from board operations
- тАЬsame object edited by multiple usersтАЭ having undefined conflict behavior

So the skill can give you a clean first 15-20 minute architecture pass.

**Big constraint for this repo**
The assignment says:
- work on `main`
- commit early and often
- push directly to `main`

That means I would **not** use a fancy branch/worktree-heavy swarm workflow here.
I would use the skill in a lighter mode:
- contract-first planning
- maybe one builder and one validator
- or at most split client/server scopes very cleanly

Not 6 parallel writers.

- `integration/persistence-and-undo-redo`

But I would still keep:

- one owner for final integration

- operation schema

- canonical shape types

- final integration stays single-owner

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

Exit code: 0 Wall time: 0.2 seconds Output: # CLAUDE.md ├втВмтАЭ Collaborative Whiteboard Take-Home ## Project Overview Real-time collaborative whiteboard for synchronous design reviews. 2-5 users on a call, same canvas, editing simultaneously. Sessions run 30-90 minutes. Canvas state persists across sessions. Source: Mechanize take-home interview assignment (3-hour time limit). ## Stack (locked ├втВмтАЭ do not swap) | Layer | Tech | |------------|-----------------------------------------| | Frontend | React 19, TypeScript 5.6, Vite 6 | | Backend | FastAPI, Python 3.12, Uvicorn | | Database | PostgreSQL 16 | | WebSocket | FastAPI built-in (Starlette) | | DB driver | asyncpg (async, uses `$1` param syntax) | | Containers | Docker Compose | Adding npm/pip packages is allowed. Swapping frameworks or DB is not. ## Repo Structure ``` ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм client/ # React frontend ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм src/ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм main.tsx # Entry point (StrictMode, createRoot) ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЭ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм App.tsx # Root component (placeholder) ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм vite.config.ts # Dev proxy: /api -> :3001, /ws -> ws://:3001 ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм tsconfig.json # strict: true, target: ES2022, jsx: react-jsx ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм package.json # type: "module" ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм index.html ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЭ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Dockerfile ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм server/ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм app/ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм main.py # FastAPI app, lifespan, /health, /ws endpoint ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм db.py # asyncpg pool singleton (get_pool / close_pool) ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЭ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм __init__.py ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм schema.sql # Runs on first PG start (currently empty) ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм pyproject.toml # deps: fastapi, uvicorn, websockets, asyncpg ├втАЭтАЪ ├втАЭтАЭ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Dockerfile ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм docker-compose.yml # db(:5432), server(:3001), client(:5173) ├втАЭ┼У├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм package.json # npm workspaces: ["client"] ├втАЭтАЭ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм package-lock.json ``` ## Current State (Scaffold Only) Everything below is what exists today. All business logic is TODO. - **`server/app/main.py`**: FastAPI app with lifespan (init/close pool). One `GET /health` that runs `SELECT 1`. One `websocket /ws` that accepts and loops `receive_text()` but does nothing with the data. Only catches `WebSocketDisconnect`. - **`server/app/db.py`**: Module-global `asyncpg.Pool` singleton. Reads `DATABASE_URL` env var with hardcoded fallback `postgres://whiteboard:whiteboard@localhost:5432/whiteboard`. - **`server/schema.sql`**: Two comment lines. No tables. - **`client/src/App.tsx`**: Returns `
Whiteboard
`. - **`client/src/main.tsx`**: Standard React 19 entry with StrictMode. - **`client/vite.config.ts`**: Proxy config for `/api` and `/ws` to `localhost:3001`. ## Required Features (from PRD) - [ ] User accounts (signup, login, logout) - [ ] Create a canvas, open an existing canvas - [ ] Invite other users to a canvas (by username or email) - [ ] Shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, text - [ ] Select, move, resize, delete, change color - [ ] Undo / redo - [ ] See other users' cursors - [ ] Canvas state persists across sessions ## Explicitly Out of Scope - Collaborative text editing within text elements - Granular permissions (viewer/editor/owner roles) - Rich text, images, file uploads - Canvas zoom, pan, infinite canvas - Export (PNG, SVG, PDF) - Version history / named snapshots - Mobile / touch support ## Known Scaffold Issues (from audit) ### Infrastructure Bugs - **Server Dockerfile build order broken**: `pip install .` runs before source is copied. Needs restructuring. - **Client Dockerfile missing lockfile**: `npm install` without `package-lock.json` = non-deterministic builds. - **Docker networking mismatch**: Vite proxy targets `localhost:3001` which inside a container means the container itself, not the server service. Should target `http://server:3001` in Docker context. - **No DB readiness check**: `depends_on: [db]` doesn't wait for PG to accept connections. Server may crash on startup race. - **Both containers run as root**: No `USER` directive in either Dockerfile. ### Security Gaps - **Hardcoded DB credentials**: `whiteboard:whiteboard` in `db.py:7` and `docker-compose.yml:6,19`. Should use `.env` (already gitignored). - **No authentication**: Zero auth anywhere. WebSocket is open to any client. - **No CORS middleware**: Cross-origin requests from `:5173` to `:3001` will fail outside Vite proxy. - **No input validation**: WebSocket receives raw text, never parses or validates. - **No rate limiting**: No connection or message rate limits. - **Health endpoint leaks stack traces**: Unhandled DB errors return raw 500 with traceback. ### Code Quality - `data` variable in websocket handler is assigned but never used (dead code). - Only `WebSocketDisconnect` caught; other exceptions crash the handler silently. - No structured logging anywhere in the backend. ## Conventions to Follow - **Python**: Use `asyncpg` parameterized queries (`$1`, `$2`) for all DB access. Never string-format SQL. - **TypeScript**: `strict: true` is on. Do not disable it. Use `type: "module"` (ESM). - **Vite proxy**: Client talks to server via `/api/*` and `/ws` paths. Do not hardcode ports in client code. - **Docker Compose**: `schema.sql` is mounted as PG init script. Put all DDL there. - **Monorepo**: Root `package.json` uses npm workspaces with `client` as the only workspace. - **Linter**: `ruff` is configured as a dev dependency for the server. Run with `ruff check`. - **Branch**: All work on `main`. Push directly, no PRs. ## Development Commands ```bash # Docker (all services) docker compose up --build # Local dev (DB in Docker, app native) docker compose up db cd server && pip install -e . && uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 3001 cd client && npm install && npm run dev # Linting cd server && ruff check . # TypeScript check cd client && npx tsc --noEmit ``` ## Architecture Decisions to Make When implementing, these are the key design choices: 1. **Auth mechanism**: JWT tokens (stateless) vs session cookies. JWT is simpler for WebSocket auth (pass token as query param on connect). 2. **WebSocket protocol**: JSON message envelope with `type` discriminator field. All board ops, cursor updates, and sync messages go through one `/ws` connection per user per canvas. 3. **Conflict resolution**: Last-write-wins is sufficient for the 2-5 user, 30-90 min session use case. No CRDT/OT needed. 4. **Undo/redo**: Per-user operation stack. Store operations in DB for persistence. Inverse operations for undo. 5. **Canvas persistence**: Save full shape state to PG. Load on canvas open. Broadcast deltas via WebSocket during session. 6. **Cursor presence**: Ephemeral, no persistence needed. Broadcast cursor positions via WebSocket at throttled rate (~50ms).

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from typing import AsyncGenerator

from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

from app.db import close_pool, get_pool


@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI) -> AsyncGenerator[None, None]:
    await get_pool()
    yield
    await close_pool()


app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)


@app.get("/health")
async def health() -> dict:
    pool = await get_pool()
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        await conn.fetchval("SELECT 1")
    return {"ok": True}


@app.websocket("/ws")
async def websocket_endpoint(ws: WebSocket) -> None:

    try:

            data = await ws.receive_text()
            # Handle incoming messages

import os

import asyncpg

DATABASE_URL = os.environ.get(
    "DATABASE_URL",
    "postgres://whiteboard:whiteboard@localhost:5432/whiteboard",
)

pool: asyncpg.Pool | None = None


async def get_pool() -> asyncpg.Pool:
    global pool
    if pool is None:
        pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(DATABASE_URL)
    return pool


async def close_pool() -> None:
    global pool
    if pool is not None:
        await pool.close()
        pool = None

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\AGENTS.md
+# AGENTS.md
+
+Use this file for scoped handoffs and post-build review in this repository. Read [CLAUDE.md](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\CLAUDE.md) first.
+
+## Non-Negotiables
+
+- Stay on the provided stack. Do not swap React, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, or Vite.
+- Work on `main`. Commit early and often. Push directly to `main`. No PR workflow.
+- Optimize for the actual deployment context: 2-5 users, same canvas, same call, simultaneous edits, 30-90 minute sessions, persistence across sessions.
+- Correctness for required features matters more than polishing out-of-scope features.
+- Keep collaborative text editing simple. Treat text edits as whole-object updates.
+- Prefer simple, explicit conflict behavior over clever distributed systems work. Last-write-wins is acceptable here.
+
+## Shared Review Priorities
+
+Every builder and reviewer should care about these first:
+
+1. Client and server agree on canvas object and websocket payload shapes.
+2. Undo and redo semantics are internally consistent and do not corrupt persisted state.
+3. Simultaneous edits to the same object have defined behavior.
+4. Presence and cursor updates are ephemeral and do not pollute persisted board state.
+5. Canvas state reloads correctly across sessions.
+6. Authentication and canvas access are enforced on both HTTP and websocket paths.
+
+## Canonical Ownership
+
+If work is split, keep ownership boundaries clean.
+
+### Agent: `infra-runtime`
+
+- Scope:
+  - `docker-compose.yml`
+  - `client/Dockerfile`
+  - `server/Dockerfile`
+  - `client/vite.config.ts`
+  - `SETUP.md`
+- Responsibilities:
+  - deterministic local startup
+  - Docker networking fixes
+  - health and readiness behavior
+  - environment-variable wiring
+- Must not own:
+  - whiteboard domain logic
+  - websocket protocol design
+  - client canvas behavior
+
+### Agent: `backend-domain`
+
+- Scope:
+  - `server/app/**`
+  - `server/schema.sql`
+- Responsibilities:
+  - auth endpoints
+  - canvas CRUD and invite flow
+  - websocket protocol
+  - persistence model
+  - undo/redo persistence semantics
+  - cursor broadcast and board event handling
+- Must not own:
+  - React component structure
+  - Vite or Docker behavior unless blocked by backend bring-up
+
+### Agent: `frontend-whiteboard`
+
+- Scope:
+  - `client/src/**`
+  - client-only package updates if required
+- Responsibilities:
+  - auth screens and session state
+  - canvas UI
+  - shape creation and manipulation
+  - remote cursor rendering
+  - websocket client integration
+  - undo and redo UX
+- Must not own:
+  - backend protocol changes without updating the shared contract first
+  - Docker or DB changes unless explicitly reassigned
+
+### Agent: `reviewer`
+
+- Scope:
+  - read-only across the whole repo
+- Responsibilities:
+  - code review after feature work
+  - identify regressions, risks, missing validation, and protocol mismatches
+  - verify required features against the take-home rubric
+- Must not do:
+  - opportunistic refactors
+  - style-only feedback unless style causes defects
+  - implementation work unless explicitly asked
+
+## Handoff Requirements
+
+Any handoff should include:
+
+- exact write scope
+- files changed
+- public exports added or changed
+- API routes added or changed
+- websocket message types added or changed
+- schema changes
+- follow-up blockers
+- what still needs validation
+
+Use this format:
+
+```md
+## Handoff
+- Scope:
+- Files changed:
+- Routes changed:
+- Websocket messages changed:
+- Schema changes:
+- New env vars:
+- Risks or blockers:
+- Next validator checks:
+```
+
+## Builder Prompt Template
+
+```text
+Read CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md first.
+Own only [write-scope].
+Implement [task].
+Preserve the provided stack and current repo conventions.
+Do not change shapes, websocket payloads, or persistence semantics outside your scope without calling it out explicitly.
+At the end, provide a handoff using the AGENTS.md format.
+```
+
+## Reviewer Prompt Template
+
+```text
+Read CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md first.
+Review the finished implementation in a code-review mindset.
+Prioritize bugs, robustness gaps, behavioral regressions, protocol mismatches, missing validation, and missing tests.
+Focus on the actual take-home requirements and the synchronous-collaboration deployment context.
+Do not fix code. Report findings first, ordered by severity, with file references.
+```
+
+## Whiteboard-Specific Review Checklist
+
+The reviewer should explicitly check:
+
+- accounts: signup, login, logout actually work
+- canvas access: create, open, invite work end to end
+- persistence: reloading a canvas restores the latest state
+- shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, and text all round-trip correctly
+- manipulation: select, move, resize, recolor, delete work locally and remotely
+- collaboration: two users see each other's edits promptly
+- cursor presence: cursors render for other users and disappear on disconnect
+- websocket auth: unauthorized users cannot attach to arbitrary canvases
+- undo and redo: behavior is predictable under concurrent edits
+- conflict handling: same-object edits do not crash or corrupt board state
+- reconnect behavior: stale or disconnected clients do not leave broken presence state behind
+
+## Recommended Review Order
+
+1. `server/schema.sql`
+2. `server/app/**` auth, canvas, invite, websocket, persistence
+3. `client/src/**` state model and websocket integration
+4. cross-check shared payload shapes between client and server
+5. Docker and local run flow
+
+## Stop Conditions
+
+Pause and escalate instead of pushing ahead when:
+
+- client and server payload shapes diverge
+- undo and redo behavior is undefined under concurrent edits
+- websocket messages are added ad hoc without a stable envelope
+- auth is implemented for HTTP but not websocket access
+- canvas persistence and live collaboration use different shape models
+
+## Notes For This Repo
+
+- The current scaffold is minimal. Expect most required behavior to be built from scratch.
+- `server/schema.sql` is the canonical place for DDL.
+- `asyncpg` uses `$1`, `$2` placeholders. Do not use string-formatted SQL.
+- The frontend should talk through `/api/*` and `/ws`, not hardcoded server ports.
+- If a contract file is introduced later, treat it as architect-owned and keep reviewers focused on contract compliance.
*** End Patch

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: whats the estimated build time for this repo

payload.message: whats the estimated build time for this repo

For this repo as it exists now, with the scaffold basically empty:

**Realistic estimate**
- `2.5 to 3 hours`: functional but narrow, if you stay very simple and use AI well
- `4 to 6 hours`: solid and defensible implementation of all required features
- `6+ hours`: robust, cleaned up, well-tested version

So for the actual Mechanize take-home, the honest answer is:

**Yes, it is possible in 3 hours, but only if you build a ruthless MVP.**

**Why**
The scaffold gives you almost nothing beyond:
- React shell
- FastAPI shell
- Postgres connection
- empty websocket loop

The expensive parts are:
- whiteboard object model
- real-time sync
- undo/redo
- auth + invites
- persistence
- client/server protocol alignment

ThatтАЩs a lot for a near-empty repo.

**My repo-specific breakdown**
- `15-20 min`: contract and architecture freeze
- `20-30 min`: schema + backend scaffolding + auth basics
- `45-60 min`: realtime board protocol + persistence
- `45-60 min`: frontend canvas/editor interactions
- `20-30 min`: invites + canvas open/create flow
- `20-30 min`: integration, bug fixing, validation

That lands around `3 to 4 hours` for a credible pass.

**What makes it fit in 3**
You need to keep these aggressively simple:
- auth: basic session/JWT flow, no polish
- invites: simplest acceptable mechanism
- conflict handling: last-write-wins
- persistence: store canonical board state, donтАЩt build elaborate versioning
- undo/redo: keep it simple and predictable, not perfect under all concurrency edge cases
- canvas: fixed viewport, no pan/zoom, no fancy UX

**What will blow the schedule**
- overengineering websocket protocol
- fancy state architecture on the frontend
- trying to make undo/redo perfect under simultaneous conflicting edits
- polishing UI
- adding unnecessary abstractions early

**My blunt estimate**
If your goal is тАЬall required features, defendable in interview,тАЭ treat this as a `3.5 hour job compressed into 3`.
If your goal is тАЬship something that passes the bar inside the window,тАЭ itтАЩs doable.

If you want, I can give you the exact 3-hour execution plan for this repo in 15-minute chunks.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: we will be running 5 parallel sessions, so thats the game.

payload.message: we will be running 5 parallel sessions, so thats the game.

That can work, but only if one session owns the spec and one session owns integration.

Five parallel builders on this repo would be a mistake. Five parallel sessions is fine if the split is:

1. `Architect / Integrator`
- Owns the contract, shared payload shapes, schema decisions, and final merge to `main`
- No one else edits shared contract files

2. `Backend Auth / Canvas Access`
- Signup, login, logout
- Create/open canvas
- Invite flow
- Access checks

3. `Backend Realtime / Persistence`
- WebSocket protocol
- Board ops
- Cursor presence
- Persistence
- Undo/redo backend semantics

4. `Frontend Whiteboard`
- Canvas rendering
- Shape tools
- Select/move/resize/delete/recolor
- Text object editing
- Undo/redo UI wiring

5. `Validator / Reviewer`
- Fresh context
- Checks client/server payload alignment
- Checks required features
- Report-only, no fixes unless explicitly reassigned

That is the right shape. Not 5 people all writing random parts.

**What happens next**
- Session 1 writes the contract pack first
- You review it quickly
- Sessions 2-4 build in parallel against that contract
- Session 5 validates while fixes happen
- Session 1 integrates and lands final changes

**Estimated wall-clock with 5 sessions**
If the contract is clean:
- `15-20 min`: contract and ownership freeze
- `45-60 min`: parallel build
- `20-30 min`: validation and fix round
- `25-40 min`: integration and bug bash

So realistic total:
- `105-150 min`

That puts the take-home in range.

**What will kill this plan**
- letting multiple sessions invent websocket payloads independently
- splitting undo/redo semantics across sessions without one owner
- letting frontend and backend each define shape models separately
- having all 5 sessions write to `main` in the same checkout

Because the repo requires work on `main`, the safest pattern is:
- one session owns `main`
- other sessions work in isolated copies/worktrees or produce scoped diffs
- integration owner lands them sequentially

**My blunt take**
With 5 well-scoped sessions, this repo becomes plausible inside 3 hours.
Without a frozen contract, 5 sessions will make you slower, not faster.

If you want, I can map the exact contract sections and write scopes IтАЩd use for those 5 sessions on this Mechanize repo.

payload.output:
Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.2 seconds
Output:
   1: # BUILD CONTRACT: Collaborative Whiteboard
   2: 
   3: ## Status
   4: - State: `planning`
   5: - Owner: architect (human review before dispatch)
   6: - Status files: **unnecessary** ├втВмтАЭ two agents, synchronous dispatch, no async handoff
   7: 
   8: ## Goal
   9: - **User outcome:** 2-5 people on a call can draw shapes on a shared canvas in real time, with undo/redo and persistence across sessions.
  10: - **Technical outcome:** React + FastAPI + PostgreSQL whiteboard with WebSocket collaboration, JWT auth, shape CRUD, cursor presence.
  11: - **Success criteria:** All 8 required features functional end-to-end. Correct under concurrent editing by 2-5 users.
  12: 
  13: ## Scope
  14: - **In:** signup/login/logout, canvas CRUD, invite by username/email, rectangle/ellipse/line/text shapes, select/move/resize/delete/recolor, undo/redo, live cursors, persistent canvas state.
  15: - **Out:** collaborative text editing within text elements, granular permissions, rich text/images/uploads, zoom/pan/infinite canvas, export, version history, mobile/touch.
  16: - **Deferred:** none ├втВмтАЭ all "in" items ship in this pass.
  17: 
  18: ## Architecture Decisions
  19: - **Stack:** React 19 + TypeScript + Vite (client), FastAPI + Python 3.12 + uvicorn (server), PostgreSQL 16. No substitutions.
  20: - **Auth:** JWT (HS256). Token in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for HTTP; in `?token=<jwt>` query param for WebSocket. `JWT_SECRET` env var. 24h expiry. Passwords hashed with bcrypt.
  21: - **Transport:** Single WebSocket at `/ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`. All shape ops and cursors flow through it. HTTP for auth + canvas CRUD only.
  22: - **Persistence:** Per-operation mutation of `shapes` table (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). No operation log. DB always holds current canvas state.
  23: - **Error model:** HTTP errors return `{ "error": "<code>" }` with appropriate status. WebSocket errors send `{ "type": "error", "message": "<text>" }`. No stack traces to client.
  24: - **Undo/Redo:** Client-side stacks only. Server sees undo as a normal operation (the inverse). Details in Undo/Redo Semantics below.
  25: 
  26: ## Shared Contract Files
  27: 
  28: | File | Role | Editor |
  29: |------|------|--------|
  30: | `BUILD_CONTRACT.md` | Human source of truth | Architect only |
  31: | `client/src/types.ts` | Machine source of truth (canonical) | Architect only |
  32: | `server/app/types.py` | Python mirror of TS types | Architect only |
  33: 
  34: Builders import from these files. They never redefine shared types locally.
  35: 
  36: ---
  37: 
  38: ## Frozen Schemas
  39: 
  40: ### Shape Schema
  41: 
  42: ```typescript
  43: interface Shape {
  44:   id: string;            // UUIDv4
  45:   type: "rectangle" | "ellipse" | "line" | "text";
  46:   x: number;             // bounding-box left  (line: startX)
  47:   y: number;             // bounding-box top   (line: startY)
  48:   width: number;         // bounding-box width (line: endX ├в╦ЖтАЩ startX)
  49:   height: number;        // bounding-box height(line: endY ├в╦ЖтАЩ startY)
  50:   fill: string;          // "#rrggbb" or "" for none
  51:   stroke: string;        // "#rrggbb"
  52:   strokeWidth: number;   // >= 1
  53:   text?: string;         // required when type === "text"
  54:   fontSize?: number;     // required when type === "text", default 16
  55: }
  56: ```
  57: 
  58: All shapes share the bounding-box model. Line uses `(x,y)` as start and `(x+width, y+height)` as end.
  59: 
  60: ### Board Operation Protocol
  61: 
  62: ```typescript
  63: type Operation =
  64:   | { kind: "add"; shape: Shape }
  65:   | { kind: "update"; shapeId: string; props: Partial<ShapeProps> }
  66:   | { kind: "delete"; shapeId: string };
  67: ```
  68: 
  69: - `add` ├втАатАЩ server INSERTs shape, broadcasts to other clients.
  70: - `update` ├втАатАЩ server UPDATEs changed columns, broadcasts to others.
  71: - `delete` ├втАатАЩ server DELETEs row, broadcasts to others.
  72: - Op on non-existent shape ├втАатАЩ server silently ignores, no error.
  73: - `ShapeProps` = all Shape fields except `id` and `type` (immutable after creation).
  74: 
  75: ### WebSocket Event Protocol
  76: 
  77: **Connection:** `WS /ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`
  78: 
  79: Server validates JWT and canvas membership before accepting. Rejects with close code 4001 (auth) or 4003 (access).
  80: 
  81: **Client ├втАатАЩ Server:**
  82: ```typescript
  83: | { type: "op"; op: Operation }
  84: | { type: "cursor"; x: number; y: number }
  85: ```
  86: 
  87: **Server ├втАатАЩ Client:**
  88: ```typescript
  89: | { type: "init"; shapes: Shape[]; users: PresenceUser[] }   // on connect
  90: | { type: "op"; op: Operation; userId: string }               // from another user
  91: | { type: "cursor"; userId: string; username: string; x: number; y: number }
  92: | { type: "join"; user: PresenceUser }
  93: | { type: "leave"; userId: string }
  94: | { type: "error"; message: string }
  95: ```
  96: 
  97: **Flow:**
  98: 1. Client connects ├втАатАЩ server sends `init` with all shapes + online users.
  99: 2. Client sends `op` ├втАатАЩ server persists, broadcasts `op` to **all other** clients on that canvas. Sender already applied optimistically.
 100: 3. Client sends `cursor` ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `cursor` to all other clients. Not persisted.
 101: 4. On connect/disconnect ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `join`/`leave`.
 102: 
 103: ### Cursor Presence Protocol
 104: 
 105: - Server holds in-memory `Map<canvasId, Map<userId, {username, color, ws}>>`.
 106: - On connect: assign color from `CURSOR_PALETTE` (round-robin by index), broadcast `join`.
 107: - On `cursor` message: broadcast to other clients on same canvas.
 108: - On disconnect: remove from map, broadcast `leave`.

 113: Client-only. Server has no concept of undo.

 121: Client stacks: `undoStack: UndoEntry[]`, `redoStack: UndoEntry[]`.
 122: - **Do:** push `{forward, reverse}` onto undoStack, clear redoStack, send forward to server.
 123: - **Undo:** pop from undoStack, push to redoStack, send reverse to server.
 124: - **Redo:** pop from redoStack, push to undoStack, send forward to server.

# BUILD CONTRACT: Collaborative Whiteboard

## Status
- State: `planning`
- Owner: architect (human review before dispatch)
- Status files: **unnecessary** ├втВмтАЭ two agents, synchronous dispatch, no async handoff

## Goal
- **User outcome:** 2-5 people on a call can draw shapes on a shared canvas in real time, with undo/redo and persistence across sessions.
- **Technical outcome:** React + FastAPI + PostgreSQL whiteboard with WebSocket collaboration, JWT auth, shape CRUD, cursor presence.
- **Success criteria:** All 8 required features functional end-to-end. Correct under concurrent editing by 2-5 users.

## Scope
- **In:** signup/login/logout, canvas CRUD, invite by username/email, rectangle/ellipse/line/text shapes, select/move/resize/delete/recolor, undo/redo, live cursors, persistent canvas state.
- **Out:** collaborative text editing within text elements, granular permissions, rich text/images/uploads, zoom/pan/infinite canvas, export, version history, mobile/touch.
- **Deferred:** none ├втВмтАЭ all "in" items ship in this pass.

## Architecture Decisions
- **Stack:** React 19 + TypeScript + Vite (client), FastAPI + Python 3.12 + uvicorn (server), PostgreSQL 16. No substitutions.
- **Auth:** JWT (HS256). Token in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for HTTP; in `?token=<jwt>` query param for WebSocket. `JWT_SECRET` env var. 24h expiry. Passwords hashed with bcrypt.
- **Transport:** Single WebSocket at `/ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`. All shape ops and cursors flow through it. HTTP for auth + canvas CRUD only.
- **Persistence:** Per-operation mutation of `shapes` table (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). No operation log. DB always holds current canvas state.
- **Error model:** HTTP errors return `{ "error": "<code>" }` with appropriate status. WebSocket errors send `{ "type": "error", "message": "<text>" }`. No stack traces to client.
- **Undo/Redo:** Client-side stacks only. Server sees undo as a normal operation (the inverse). Details in Undo/Redo Semantics below.

## Shared Contract Files

| File | Role | Editor |
|------|------|--------|
| `BUILD_CONTRACT.md` | Human source of truth | Architect only |
| `client/src/types.ts` | Machine source of truth (canonical) | Architect only |
| `server/app/types.py` | Python mirror of TS types | Architect only |

Builders import from these files. They never redefine shared types locally.

---

## Frozen Schemas

### Shape Schema

```typescript
interface Shape {
  id: string;            // UUIDv4
  type: "rectangle" | "ellipse" | "line" | "text";
  x: number;             // bounding-box left  (line: startX)
  y: number;             // bounding-box top   (line: startY)
  width: number;         // bounding-box width (line: endX ├в╦ЖтАЩ startX)
  height: number;        // bounding-box height(line: endY ├в╦ЖтАЩ startY)
  fill: string;          // "#rrggbb" or "" for none
  stroke: string;        // "#rrggbb"
  strokeWidth: number;   // >= 1
  text?: string;         // required when type === "text"
  fontSize?: number;     // required when type === "text", default 16
}
```

All shapes share the bounding-box model. Line uses `(x,y)` as start and `(x+width, y+height)` as end.

### Board Operation Protocol

```typescript
type Operation =
  | { kind: "add"; shape: Shape }
  | { kind: "update"; shapeId: string; props: Partial<ShapeProps> }
  | { kind: "delete"; shapeId: string };
```

- `add` ├втАатАЩ server INSERTs shape, broadcasts to other clients.
- `update` ├втАатАЩ server UPDATEs changed columns, broadcasts to others.
- `delete` ├втАатАЩ server DELETEs row, broadcasts to others.
- Op on non-existent shape ├втАатАЩ server silently ignores, no error.
- `ShapeProps` = all Shape fields except `id` and `type` (immutable after creation).

### WebSocket Event Protocol

**Connection:** `WS /ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`

Server validates JWT and canvas membership before accepting. Rejects with close code 4001 (auth) or 4003 (access).

**Client ├втАатАЩ Server:**
```typescript
| { type: "op"; op: Operation }
| { type: "cursor"; x: number; y: number }
```

**Server ├втАатАЩ Client:**
```typescript
| { type: "init"; shapes: Shape[]; users: PresenceUser[] }   // on connect
| { type: "op"; op: Operation; userId: string }               // from another user
| { type: "cursor"; userId: string; username: string; x: number; y: number }
| { type: "join"; user: PresenceUser }
| { type: "leave"; userId: string }
| { type: "error"; message: string }
```

**Flow:**
1. Client connects ├втАатАЩ server sends `init` with all shapes + online users.
2. Client sends `op` ├втАатАЩ server persists, broadcasts `op` to **all other** clients on that canvas. Sender already applied optimistically.
3. Client sends `cursor` ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `cursor` to all other clients. Not persisted.
4. On connect/disconnect ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `join`/`leave`.

### Cursor Presence Protocol

- Server holds in-memory `Map<canvasId, Map<userId, {username, color, ws}>>`.
- On connect: assign color from `CURSOR_PALETTE` (round-robin by index), broadcast `join`.
- On `cursor` message: broadcast to other clients on same canvas.
- On disconnect: remove from map, broadcast `leave`.
- Cursors are **never persisted**. Presence map is purely in-memory.

### Undo/Redo Semantics

Client-only. Server has no concept of undo.

| Action | Forward op | Reverse op |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Add shape S | `{kind:"add", shape:S}` | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` |
| Delete shape S | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` | `{kind:"add", shape:<snapshot of S>}` |
| Update shape S props | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<new>}` | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<old>}` |

Client stacks: `undoStack: UndoEntry[]`, `redoStack: UndoEntry[]`.
- **Do:** push `{forward, reverse}` onto undoStack, clear redoStack, send forward to server.
- **Undo:** pop from undoStack, push to redoStack, send reverse to server.
- **Redo:** pop from redoStack, push to undoStack, send forward to server.

### Persistence Boundary

**Persisted (PostgreSQL):**
- Users, canvases, canvas memberships, shapes (current state).

**Not persisted (ephemeral):**
- Cursor positions (server in-memory)
- Undo/redo stacks (client in-memory)
- Operation history (not stored)
- Online presence (server in-memory)

### Database Schema

Server builder writes this to `server/schema.sql`. Exact DDL:

```sql
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgcrypto;

CREATE TABLE users (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    username    TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    email       TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    password_hash TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE canvases (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name        TEXT NOT NULL,
    owner_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE canvas_members (
    canvas_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    user_id     UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    PRIMARY KEY (canvas_id, user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE shapes (
    id           UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    canvas_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    type         TEXT NOT NULL,
    x            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    y            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    width        DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    height       DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    fill         TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
    stroke       TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '#000000',
    stroke_width DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 2,
    text         TEXT,
    font_size    DOUBLE PRECISION
);

CREATE INDEX idx_shapes_canvas ON shapes(canvas_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_canvas_members_user ON canvas_members(user_id);
```
