10. Optimistic updates: apply ops locally, send to server, render remote ops on receive.

Import types from ./types.ts. Never redefine shared types.
Use Vite proxy for API/WS in dev (already configured).
No framework swaps. Add npm packages as needed.
```

### Validator

```
Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md, validation-instructions.md, client/src/types.ts, server/app/types.py.
Start fresh ├втВмтАЭ no builder context.
Validate each module (server, client) against the contract.
Output PASS or FAIL per check with evidence and required fix.
Do not modify code.
```

### Integration

```
Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md and both validated modules.
Wire together only at the HTTP/WS boundary (no changes to internal logic).
Verify: signup ├втАатАЩ login ├втАатАЩ create canvas ├втАатАЩ invite second user ├втАатАЩ both connect WS ├втАатАЩ
draw shapes ├втАатАЩ see on other client ├втАатАЩ undo ├втАатАЩ refresh ├втАатАЩ shapes persist.
Only this step may edit across ownership boundaries.
```

"""
Shared contract types ├втВмтАЭ Python mirror of client/src/types.ts.
Canonical source of truth is the TypeScript file.
READ-ONLY after dispatch. Request architect change if needed.

All TypedDict keys use camelCase to match the JSON wire format.
DB columns use snake_case; the server maps between them.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Literal, TypedDict

# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Shape Schema ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм

ShapeType = Literal["rectangle", "ellipse", "line", "text"]


class _ShapeRequired(TypedDict):
    id: str
    type: ShapeType
    x: float
    y: float
    width: float
    height: float
    fill: str
    stroke: str
    strokeWidth: float


class Shape(_ShapeRequired, total=False):
    """Optional fields present only for type == 'text'."""
    text: str
    fontSize: float


# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Operations ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


class AddOp(TypedDict):
    kind: Literal["add"]
    shape: Shape


class UpdateOp(TypedDict):
    kind: Literal["update"]
    shapeId: str
    props: dict  # Partial<ShapeProps> ├втВмтАЭ validated at runtime


class DeleteOp(TypedDict):
    kind: Literal["delete"]
    shapeId: str


Operation = AddOp | UpdateOp | DeleteOp

# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм WebSocket Messages ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


class PresenceUser(TypedDict):
    userId: str
    username: str
    color: str


# Client ├втАатАЩ Server

class OpClientMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["op"]
    op: Operation


class CursorClientMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["cursor"]
    x: float
    y: float


ClientMessage = OpClientMsg | CursorClientMsg

# Server ├втАатАЩ Client


class InitServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["init"]
    shapes: list[Shape]
    users: list[PresenceUser]


class OpServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["op"]
    op: Operation
    userId: str


class CursorServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["cursor"]
    userId: str
    username: str
    x: float
    y: float


class JoinServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["join"]
    user: PresenceUser


class LeaveServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["leave"]
    userId: str


class ErrorServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["error"]
    message: str


ServerMessage = (
    InitServerMsg | OpServerMsg | CursorServerMsg
    | JoinServerMsg | LeaveServerMsg | ErrorServerMsg
)

# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Auth API ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


class AuthUser(TypedDict):
    id: str
    username: str
    email: str


class SignupRequest(TypedDict):
    username: str
    email: str
    password: str


class LoginRequest(TypedDict):
    username: str
    password: str


class AuthResponse(TypedDict):
    token: str
    user: AuthUser


# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Canvas API ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


class CanvasSummary(TypedDict):
    id: str
    name: str
    ownerId: str
    createdAt: str


class CanvasDetail(CanvasSummary, total=False):
    shapes: list[Shape]
    members: list[dict]  # [{ userId, username }]


class CreateCanvasRequest(TypedDict):
    name: str


class InviteRequest(TypedDict):
    identifier: str


# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Helpers ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм

payload.message:
Not yet. ItтАЩs close enough to start from, but I would fix these before dispatch.

- `[P1]` The websocket contract does not define an authoritative operation order for concurrent same-object edits. In [BUILD_CONTRACT.md](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\BUILD_CONTRACT.md#L97), the sender applies optimistically and the server broadcasts only to other clients. That means two users editing the same shape can diverge depending on message arrival order. You need either server-assigned revisions/sequence numbers or the server echoing canonical ops to all clients, including the sender.

- `[P1]` Undo/redo is underspecified for concurrent edits and can overwrite later remote changes. In [BUILD_CONTRACT.md](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\BUILD_CONTRACT.md#L111), undo is just тАЬsend the inverse op.тАЭ If user A changes color, user B moves/resizes, then user A undoes, the current spec does not say how to avoid clobbering newer state. You need an explicit rule like тАЬundo replays only the fields changed by that local op against latest stateтАЭ or тАЬundo is best-effort LWW and may overwrite same-field remote edits.тАЭ

- `[P1]` This contract does not match your stated 5-session plan. In [BUILD_CONTRACT.md](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\BUILD_CONTRACT.md#L6) and [BUILD_CONTRACT.md](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\BUILD_CONTRACT.md#L213), it is explicitly a 2-builder split: one `server/`, one `client/src/`. If you launch 5 sessions against this, they will either overlap or ignore the contract. You need a finer ownership map before parallel dispatch.

- `[P2]` The тАЬshared contractтАЭ is still duplicated across languages, which raises drift risk during fixes and integration. [BUILD_CONTRACT.md](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\BUILD_CONTRACT.md#L30) says `client/src/types.ts` is canonical and `server/app/types.py` is a mirror, which is workable, but only if the architect is the sole editor forever. With multiple sessions moving fast, this is fragile. At minimum, the validator should treat any mismatch between those files as an immediate fail.

If you were only doing a 2-session client/server split, IтАЩd call this mostly usable after fixing the websocket ordering issue. For your actual 5-session plan, I would not dispatch yet.

What IтАЩd change next:
- Split ownership into `infra-runtime`, `backend-auth-canvas`, `backend-realtime-persistence`, `frontend-whiteboard`, `validator`.
- Add a canonical ordering rule for ops.
- Tighten undo/redo semantics under concurrent edits.
- Make validator explicitly compare `client/src/types.ts` and `server/app/types.py`.

If you want, I can tell you exactly how IтАЩd rewrite the ownership map and the op-ordering section.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

   1: # BUILD CONTRACT: Collaborative Whiteboard
   2: 
   3: ## Status
   4: - State: `planning`
   5: - Owner: architect (human review before dispatch)
   6: - Status files: **unnecessary** ├втВмтАЭ two agents, synchronous dispatch, no async handoff
   7: 
   8: ## Goal
   9: - **User outcome:** 2-5 people on a call can draw shapes on a shared canvas in real time, with undo/redo and persistence across sessions.
  10: - **Technical outcome:** React + FastAPI + PostgreSQL whiteboard with WebSocket collaboration, JWT auth, shape CRUD, cursor presence.
  11: - **Success criteria:** All 8 required features functional end-to-end. Correct under concurrent editing by 2-5 users.
  12: 
  13: ## Scope
  14: - **In:** signup/login/logout, canvas CRUD, invite by username/email, rectangle/ellipse/line/text shapes, select/move/resize/delete/recolor, undo/redo, live cursors, persistent canvas state.
  15: - **Out:** collaborative text editing within text elements, granular permissions, rich text/images/uploads, zoom/pan/infinite canvas, export, version history, mobile/touch.
  16: - **Deferred:** none ├втВмтАЭ all "in" items ship in this pass.
  17: 
  18: ## Architecture Decisions
  19: - **Stack:** React 19 + TypeScript + Vite (client), FastAPI + Python 3.12 + uvicorn (server), PostgreSQL 16. No substitutions.
  20: - **Auth:** JWT (HS256). Token in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for HTTP; in `?token=<jwt>` query param for WebSocket. `JWT_SECRET` env var. 24h expiry. Passwords hashed with bcrypt.
  21: - **Transport:** Single WebSocket at `/ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`. All shape ops and cursors flow through it. HTTP for auth + canvas CRUD only.
  22: - **Persistence:** Per-operation mutation of `shapes` table (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). No operation log. DB always holds current canvas state.
  23: - **Error model:** HTTP errors return `{ "error": "<code>" }` with appropriate status. WebSocket errors send `{ "type": "error", "message": "<text>" }`. No stack traces to client.
  24: - **Undo/Redo:** Client-side stacks only. Server sees undo as a normal operation (the inverse). Details in Undo/Redo Semantics below.
  25: 
  26: ## Shared Contract Files
  27: 
  28: | File | Role | Editor |
  29: |------|------|--------|
  30: | `BUILD_CONTRACT.md` | Human source of truth | Architect only |
  31: | `client/src/types.ts` | Machine source of truth (canonical) | Architect only |
  32: | `server/app/types.py` | Python mirror of TS types | Architect only |
  33: 
  34: Builders import from these files. They never redefine shared types locally.
  35: 
  36: ---
  37: 
  38: ## Frozen Schemas
  39: 
  40: ### Shape Schema
  41: 
  42: ```typescript
  43: interface Shape {
  44:   id: string;            // UUIDv4
  45:   type: "rectangle" | "ellipse" | "line" | "text";
  46:   x: number;             // bounding-box left  (line: startX)
  47:   y: number;             // bounding-box top   (line: startY)
  48:   width: number;         // bounding-box width (line: endX ├в╦ЖтАЩ startX)
  49:   height: number;        // bounding-box height(line: endY ├в╦ЖтАЩ startY)
  50:   fill: string;          // "#rrggbb" or "" for none
  51:   stroke: string;        // "#rrggbb"
  52:   strokeWidth: number;   // >= 1
  53:   text?: string;         // required when type === "text"
  54:   fontSize?: number;     // required when type === "text", default 16
  55: }
  56: ```
  57: 
  58: All shapes share the bounding-box model. Line uses `(x,y)` as start and `(x+width, y+height)` as end.
  59: 
  60: ### Board Operation Protocol
  61: 
  62: ```typescript
  63: type Operation =
  64:   | { kind: "add"; shape: Shape }
  65:   | { kind: "update"; shapeId: string; props: Partial<ShapeProps> }
  66:   | { kind: "delete"; shapeId: string };
  67: ```
  68: 
  69: - `add` ├втАатАЩ server INSERTs shape, broadcasts to other clients.
  70: - `update` ├втАатАЩ server UPDATEs changed columns, broadcasts to others.
  71: - `delete` ├втАатАЩ server DELETEs row, broadcasts to others.
  72: - Op on non-existent shape ├втАатАЩ server silently ignores, no error.
  73: - `ShapeProps` = all Shape fields except `id` and `type` (immutable after creation).
  74: 
  75: ### WebSocket Event Protocol
  76: 
  77: **Connection:** `WS /ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`
  78: 
  79: Server validates JWT and canvas membership before accepting. Rejects with close code 4001 (auth) or 4003 (access).
  80: 
  81: **Client ├втАатАЩ Server:**
  82: ```typescript
  83: | { type: "op"; op: Operation }
  84: | { type: "cursor"; x: number; y: number }
  85: ```
  86: 
  87: **Server ├втАатАЩ Client:**
  88: ```typescript
  89: | { type: "init"; shapes: Shape[]; users: PresenceUser[] }   // on connect
  90: | { type: "op"; op: Operation; userId: string }               // from another user
  91: | { type: "cursor"; userId: string; username: string; x: number; y: number }
  92: | { type: "join"; user: PresenceUser }
  93: | { type: "leave"; userId: string }
  94: | { type: "error"; message: string }
  95: ```
  96: 
  97: **Flow:**
  98: 1. Client connects ├втАатАЩ server sends `init` with all shapes + online users.
  99: 2. Client sends `op` ├втАатАЩ server persists, broadcasts `op` to **all other** clients on that canvas. Sender already applied optimistically.
 100: 3. Client sends `cursor` ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `cursor` to all other clients. Not persisted.
 101: 4. On connect/disconnect ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `join`/`leave`.
 102: 
 103: ### Cursor Presence Protocol
 104: 
 105: - Server holds in-memory `Map<canvasId, Map<userId, {username, color, ws}>>`.
 106: - On connect: assign color from `CURSOR_PALETTE` (round-robin by index), broadcast `join`.
 107: - On `cursor` message: broadcast to other clients on same canvas.
 108: - On disconnect: remove from map, broadcast `leave`.
 109: - Cursors are **never persisted**. Presence map is purely in-memory.
 110: 
 111: ### Undo/Redo Semantics
 112: 
 113: Client-only. Server has no concept of undo.
 114: 
 115: | Action | Forward op | Reverse op |
 116: |--------|-----------|------------|
 117: | Add shape S | `{kind:"add", shape:S}` | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` |
 118: | Delete shape S | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` | `{kind:"add", shape:<snapshot of S>}` |
 119: | Update shape S props | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<new>}` | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<old>}` |
 120: 
 121: Client stacks: `undoStack: UndoEntry[]`, `redoStack: UndoEntry[]`.
 122: - **Do:** push `{forward, reverse}` onto undoStack, clear redoStack, send forward to server.
 123: - **Undo:** pop from undoStack, push to redoStack, send reverse to server.
 124: - **Redo:** pop from redoStack, push to undoStack, send forward to server.
 125: 
 126: ### Persistence Boundary
 127: 
 128: **Persisted (PostgreSQL):**
 129: - Users, canvases, canvas memberships, shapes (current state).
 130: 
 131: **Not persisted (ephemeral):**
 132: - Cursor positions (server in-memory)
 133: - Undo/redo stacks (client in-memory)
 134: - Operation history (not stored)
 135: - Online presence (server in-memory)
 136: 
 137: ### Database Schema
 138: 
 139: Server builder writes this to `server/schema.sql`. Exact DDL:
 140: 
 141: ```sql
 142: CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgcrypto;
 143: 
 144: CREATE TABLE users (
 145:     id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
 146:     username    TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
 147:     email       TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
 148:     password_hash TEXT NOT NULL,
 149:     created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
 150: );
 151: 
 152: CREATE TABLE canvases (
 153:     id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
 154:     name        TEXT NOT NULL,
 155:     owner_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
 156:     created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
 157: );
 158: 
 159: CREATE TABLE canvas_members (
 160:     canvas_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
 161:     user_id     UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
 162:     PRIMARY KEY (canvas_id, user_id)
 163: );
 164: 
 165: CREATE TABLE shapes (
 166:     id           UUID PRIMARY KEY,
 167:     canvas_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
 168:     type         TEXT NOT NULL,
 169:     x            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
 170:     y            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
 171:     width        DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
 172:     height       DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
 173:     fill         TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
 174:     stroke       TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '#000000',
 175:     stroke_width DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 2,
 176:     text         TEXT,
 177:     font_size    DOUBLE PRECISION
 178: );
 179: 
 180: CREATE INDEX idx_shapes_canvas ON shapes(canvas_id);
 181: CREATE INDEX idx_canvas_members_user ON canvas_members(user_id);
 182: ```
 183: 
 184: DB uses `snake_case` columns. Wire format uses `camelCase`. Server maps: `stroke_width` ├втАатАЭ `strokeWidth`, `font_size` ├втАатАЭ `fontSize`, `owner_id` ├втАатАЭ `ownerId`, `created_at` ├втАатАЭ `createdAt`.
 185: 
 186: ### Auth / Canvas / Invite API Surface
 187: 
 188: All HTTP routes under `/api`. All return JSON. Auth-protected routes require `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.
 189: 
 190: **Auth:**
 191: 
 192: | Method | Path | Body | Response | Errors |
 193: |--------|------|------|----------|--------|
 194: | POST | `/api/auth/signup` | `SignupRequest` | `AuthResponse` | 409 `username_taken`, `email_taken` |
 195: | POST | `/api/auth/login` | `LoginRequest` | `AuthResponse` | 401 `invalid_credentials` |
 196: | GET | `/api/auth/me` | ├втВмтАЭ | `AuthUser` | 401 `unauthorized` |
 197: 
 198: **Canvas:**
 199: 
 200: | Method | Path | Body | Response | Errors |
 201: |--------|------|------|----------|--------|
 202: | GET | `/api/canvases` | ├втВмтАЭ | `CanvasSummary[]` | 401 |
 203: | POST | `/api/canvases` | `CreateCanvasRequest` | `CanvasSummary` | 401 |
 204: | GET | `/api/canvases/:id` | ├втВмтАЭ | `CanvasDetail` | 401, 403 `not_a_member`, 404 `not_found` |
 205: | POST | `/api/canvases/:id/invite` | `InviteRequest` | `{ ok: true }` | 401, 403 `not_a_member`, 404 `user_not_found` / `canvas_not_found` |
 206: 
 207: Canvas creator is automatically added to `canvas_members`. `GET /api/canvases` returns all canvases where user is a member.
 208: 
 209: ---
 210: 
 211: ## Ownership Map
 212: 
 213: | Module | Owner | Write Scope | Blocked By | Done When |
 214: |--------|-------|-------------|------------|-----------|
 215: | **server** | server-builder | `server/` (all files) | contract frozen | Health check passes, all HTTP endpoints return correct shapes, WS connects and broadcasts ops + cursors, shapes persist across restart |
 216: | **client** | client-builder | `client/src/` (all files) | contract frozen | Auth flow works, canvas list/create/invite UI, shape drawing + editing on canvas, undo/redo, live cursors visible, state reloads on refresh |
 217: 
 218: **Integration** (after both pass validation): wire together, run full E2E: signup ├втАатАЩ create canvas ├втАатАЩ invite ├втАатАЩ draw shapes ├втАатАЩ verify on second browser ├втАатАЩ undo ├втАатАЩ refresh ├втАатАЩ verify persistence.
 219: 
 220: ## Naming Rules
 221: 
 222: **Server (Python):**
 223: - Files: `snake_case.py` in `server/app/`
 224: - Functions: `snake_case` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `create_user`, `get_canvas`
 225: - Route handlers: `snake_case` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `signup`, `login`, `list_canvases`
 226: - Route prefixes: auth routes in `auth.py` (or router), canvas routes in `canvas.py`
 227: - Imports: absolute from `app` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `from app.types import Shape`
 228: 
 229: **Client (TypeScript):**
 230: - Files: `PascalCase.tsx` for components, `camelCase.ts` for non-components
 231: - Functions/hooks: `camelCase` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `useAuth`, `useCanvas`, `useWebSocket`
 232: - Components: `PascalCase` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `Canvas`, `Toolbar`, `LoginForm`
 233: - Types: imported from `./types.ts`, never redefined locally
 234: - Imports: relative paths with `.tsx`/`.ts` extensions (per tsconfig `allowImportingTsExtensions`)
 235: 
 236: **Wire format:**
 237: - All JSON keys: `camelCase`
 238: - Error responses: `{ "error": "snake_code" }` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `"invalid_credentials"`
 239: 
 240: ## Config and Environment
 241: 
 242: | Variable | Used By | Default | Required |
 243: |----------|---------|---------|----------|
 244: | `DATABASE_URL` | server | `postgres://whiteboard:whiteboard@localhost:5432/whiteboard` | no |
 245: | `JWT_SECRET` | server | (none ├втВмтАЭ must be set or use a hardcoded dev fallback) | yes |
 246: | `VITE_WS_URL` | client | `ws://localhost:3001` | no |
 247: | `VITE_API_URL` | client | `http://localhost:3001` | no |
 248: 
 249: Server builder: add `pyjwt` and `bcrypt` (or `passlib[bcrypt]`) to `pyproject.toml` dependencies.
 250: Client builder: add packages as needed (no framework swaps).
 251: 
 252: ## Dispatch Prompts
 253: 
 254: ### Server Builder
 255: 
 256: ```
 257: Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md, server/app/types.py, and client/src/types.ts.
 258: You own only server/ (all files).
 259: 
 260: Implement in this order:
 261: 1. Write schema.sql (exact DDL from contract).
 262: 2. Auth: signup, login, me endpoints with JWT + bcrypt.
 263: 3. Canvas: CRUD + invite endpoints with membership checks.
 264: 4. WebSocket: /ws handler with auth, init, op broadcast, cursor broadcast, join/leave presence.
 265: 5. CORS middleware allowing the client origin.
 266: 6. Wire all routers into main.py.
 267: 
 268: Import types from app.types. Never redefine shared types.
 269: Map snake_case DB columns ├втАатАЭ camelCase wire format.
 270: Ops on non-existent shapes are silently ignored.
 271: Preserve existing db.py pool pattern.
 272: Add pyjwt and bcrypt to pyproject.toml.
 273: ```
 274: 
 275: ### Client Builder
 276: 
 277: ```
 278: Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md, client/src/types.ts, and server/app/types.py.
 279: You own only client/src/ (all files).
 280: 
 281: Implement:
 282: 1. Auth: signup/login forms, token storage (localStorage), logout, protected routes.
 283: 2. Canvas list: show user's canvases, create new, navigate to canvas.
 284: 3. Invite: simple form on canvas page to invite by username/email.
 285: 4. Canvas rendering: HTML5 Canvas or SVG. Render all shape types.
 286: 5. Tools: rectangle, ellipse, line, text tool. Current tool selector + color picker.
 287: 6. Interactions: select (click), move (drag), resize (drag handles), delete (key), recolor.
 288: 7. Undo/redo: client-side stacks per UndoEntry contract. Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Shift+Z.
 289: 8. WebSocket client: connect on canvas open, handle all ServerMessage types.
 290: 9. Live cursors: render other users' cursors with username label + assigned color.
 291: 10. Optimistic updates: apply ops locally, send to server, render remote ops on receive.
 292: 
 293: Import types from ./types.ts. Never redefine shared types.
 294: Use Vite proxy for API/WS in dev (already configured).
 295: No framework swaps. Add npm packages as needed.
 296: ```
 297: 
 298: ### Validator
 299: 
 300: ```
 301: Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md, validation-instructions.md, client/src/types.ts, server/app/types.py.
 302: Start fresh ├втВмтАЭ no builder context.
 303: Validate each module (server, client) against the contract.
 304: Output PASS or FAIL per check with evidence and required fix.
 305: Do not modify code.
 306: ```
 307: 
 308: ### Integration
 309: 
 310: ```
 311: Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md and both validated modules.
 312: Wire together only at the HTTP/WS boundary (no changes to internal logic).
 313: Verify: signup ├втАатАЩ login ├втАатАЩ create canvas ├втАатАЩ invite second user ├втАатАЩ both connect WS ├втАатАЩ
 314: draw shapes ├втАатАЩ see on other client ├втАатАЩ undo ├втАатАЩ refresh ├втАатАЩ shapes persist.
 315: Only this step may edit across ownership boundaries.
 316: ```
 317: 
 318: ## Open Questions
 319: 
 320: None ├втВмтАЭ contract is complete for the required feature set.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: how fast can you edit with multi agents? enable sub agents

payload.message: how fast can you edit with multi agents? enable sub agents

I can move faster with sub-agents, but only if you explicitly want delegation and parallel agent work.

You just did: тАЬenable sub agents.тАЭ So yes, I can use them.

What it buys you:
- parallel codebase reading
- parallel scoped implementation
- parallel validation
- faster turnaround when scopes are clean

What it does not fix:
- bad contract
- overlapping write scopes
- unclear ownership
- integration bottlenecks

For this repo, sub-agents are useful if I split work like:
- one agent on backend auth/canvas API
- one agent on backend websocket/persistence
- one agent on frontend whiteboard UI
- one agent on review/validation

But I would still keep:
- one contract owner
- one integration owner

So yes, I can enable and use sub-agents now. The speed gain is real when the work is partitioned correctly. If the spec is still moving, parallelism mostly creates merge pain.

When you want me to actually use them, say the word and IтАЩll structure the delegation around the current contract.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.2 seconds
Output:
# BUILD CONTRACT: Collaborative Whiteboard

## Status
- State: `planning`
- Owner: architect (human review before dispatch)
- Status files: **unnecessary** ├втВмтАЭ 5 sessions with clear phase gates, no async handoff

## Goal
- **User outcome:** 2-5 people on a call can draw shapes on a shared canvas in real time, with undo/redo and persistence across sessions.
- **Technical outcome:** React + FastAPI + PostgreSQL whiteboard with WebSocket collaboration, JWT auth, shape CRUD, cursor presence, server-authoritative operation ordering.
- **Success criteria:** All 8 required features functional end-to-end. Concurrent same-object edits converge to identical state on all clients.

## Scope
- **In:** signup/login/logout, canvas CRUD, invite by username/email, rectangle/ellipse/line/text shapes, select/move/resize/delete/recolor, undo/redo, live cursors, persistent canvas state.
- **Out:** collaborative text editing within text elements, granular permissions, rich text/images/uploads, zoom/pan/infinite canvas, export, version history, mobile/touch.
- **Deferred:** none ├втВмтАЭ all "in" items ship in this pass.

## Architecture Decisions
- **Stack:** React 19 + TypeScript + Vite (client), FastAPI + Python 3.12 + uvicorn (server), PostgreSQL 16. No substitutions.
- **Auth:** JWT (HS256). Token in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for HTTP; in `?token=<jwt>` query param for WebSocket. `JWT_SECRET` env var. 24h expiry. Passwords hashed with bcrypt.
- **Transport:** Single WebSocket at `/ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`. All shape ops and cursors flow through it. HTTP for auth + canvas CRUD only.
- **Ordering:** Server-authoritative. Server assigns a per-canvas monotonic `seq` to each op and echoes to ALL clients (including sender). Clients converge by applying ops in `seq` order. Details in ├В┬зWebSocket Event Protocol.
- **Concurrency model:** LWW (last-writer-wins) at field level, ordered by server arrival. No OT, no CRDTs.
- **Persistence:** Per-operation mutation of `shapes` table (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). No operation log. DB always holds current canvas state.
- **Error model:** HTTP errors return `{ "error": "<code>" }` with appropriate status. WebSocket errors send `{ "type": "error", "message": "<text>" }`. No stack traces to client.
- **Undo/Redo:** Client-side stacks. Server sees undo as a normal operation (the inverse). Field-level: only the originally changed fields are reversed. Details in ├В┬зUndo/Redo Semantics.

## Shared Contract Files

| File | Role | Editor |
|------|------|--------|
| `BUILD_CONTRACT.md` | Human source of truth | Architect only |
| `client/src/types.ts` | Machine source of truth (canonical) | Architect only |
| `server/app/types.py` | Python mirror of TS types | Architect only |

Builders import from these files. They never redefine shared types locally. Validator MUST check parity between `types.ts` and `types.py` ├втВмтАЭ any mismatch is an immediate FAIL.

---

## Frozen Schemas

### Shape Schema

```typescript
interface Shape {
  id: string;            // UUIDv4
  type: "rectangle" | "ellipse" | "line" | "text";
  x: number;             // bounding-box left  (line: startX)
  y: number;             // bounding-box top   (line: startY)
  width: number;         // bounding-box width (line: endX ├в╦ЖтАЩ startX)
  height: number;        // bounding-box height(line: endY ├в╦ЖтАЩ startY)
  fill: string;          // "#rrggbb" or "" for none
  stroke: string;        // "#rrggbb"
  strokeWidth: number;   // >= 1
  text?: string;         // required when type === "text"
  fontSize?: number;     // required when type === "text", default 16
}
```

All shapes share the bounding-box model. Line uses `(x,y)` as start and `(x+width, y+height)` as end.

### Board Operation Protocol

```typescript
type Operation =
  | { kind: "add"; shape: Shape }
  | { kind: "update"; shapeId: string; props: Partial<ShapeProps> }
  | { kind: "delete"; shapeId: string };
```

`ShapeProps` = all Shape fields except `id` and `type` (immutable after creation).

**Server-side processing:**
- `add` ├втАатАЩ INSERT into `shapes`. If `id` exists, silently ignore (idempotent).
- `update` ├втАатАЩ UPDATE only the columns present in `props`. If shape doesn't exist, silently ignore ├втВмтАЭ do NOT broadcast.
- `delete` ├втАатАЩ DELETE the row. If shape doesn't exist, silently ignore ├втВмтАЭ do NOT broadcast.
- Successful ops: assign `seq`, persist, broadcast to ALL clients.
- Ignored ops (non-existent target): no broadcast, no seq assigned. Client's optimistic state self-corrects on reconnect.

**Ordering guarantee:** Server processes ops for a given canvas sequentially (via per-canvas `asyncio.Lock`). This ensures `seq` is strictly monotonic and DB writes never interleave for the same canvas.

### WebSocket Event Protocol

**Connection:** `WS /ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`

Server validates JWT and canvas membership before accepting. Rejects with close code 4001 (auth failure) or 4003 (not a canvas member).

**Client ├втАатАЩ Server:**
```typescript
| { type: "op"; op: Operation; opId: string }   // opId: client-generated UUIDv4
| { type: "cursor"; x: number; y: number }
```

**Server ├втАатАЩ Client:**
```typescript
| { type: "init"; shapes: Shape[]; users: PresenceUser[]; seq: number }
| { type: "op"; op: Operation; userId: string; seq: number; opId: string }
| { type: "cursor"; userId: string; username: string; x: number; y: number }
| { type: "join"; user: PresenceUser }
| { type: "leave"; userId: string }
| { type: "error"; message: string }
```

**Server op processing flow:**
1. Receive `{type:"op", op, opId}` from client.
2. Acquire per-canvas lock.
3. Persist op to DB (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on `shapes` table).
4. If op was a no-op (target didn't exist), release lock, stop ├втВмтАЭ no broadcast.
5. Increment canvas `seq` counter. Assign `seq` to this op.
6. Release lock.
7. Broadcast `{type:"op", op, userId, seq, opId}` to **ALL** clients on the canvas, **including the sender**.

**Client optimistic reconciliation:**
1. Client generates `opId` (UUIDv4), applies op locally (optimistic), sends to server, adds `opId` to a `pendingOps` set.
2. On receiving server `op` message: if `opId` is in `pendingOps`, remove it and skip (already applied). Otherwise, apply the op to local state.
3. If server silently ignores an op (no echo received), client's optimistic state may briefly include a phantom shape. This self-corrects on reconnect (client gets fresh `init`).

**Other flows:**
- Client connects ├втАатАЩ server sends `init` with all current shapes, online users, and current `seq`.
- `cursor` messages ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts to all OTHER clients. Not persisted.
- On connect ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `join` to other clients on the canvas.
- On disconnect ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `leave`, removes from presence map.

### Cursor Presence Protocol

- Server holds in-memory `dict[str, dict[str, {username, color, ws}]]` keyed by `canvasId` ├втАатАЩ `userId`.
- On connect: assign color from `CURSOR_PALETTE` (round-robin by connection index), broadcast `join`.
- On `cursor` message: broadcast to other clients on same canvas.
- On disconnect: remove from map, broadcast `leave`.
- Cursors are **never persisted**. Presence is purely in-memory.

### Undo/Redo Semantics

**Ownership:** Client-side only. Server has no undo/redo concept ├втВмтАЭ it just processes operations.

**Stacks:** `undoStack: UndoEntry[]`, `redoStack: UndoEntry[]`.

| Local action | forward op | reverse op |
|---|---|---|
| Add shape S | `{kind:"add", shape:S}` | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` |
| Delete shape S | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` | `{kind:"add", shape:<snapshot of S before delete>}` |
| Update shape S fields F | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<new values of F>}` | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<old values of F>}` |

**Stack operations:**
- **Do:** push `{forward, reverse}` onto undoStack, clear redoStack, apply forward optimistically, send forward to server.
- **Undo:** pop from undoStack, push to redoStack, apply reverse optimistically, send reverse to server.
- **Redo:** pop from redoStack, push to undoStack, apply forward optimistically, send forward to server.

**Concurrent-edit behavior (field-level LWW):**
- Update reverse ops contain ONLY the fields from the original op. Example: user A changed `fill` ├втАатАЩ reverse is `{props: {fill: oldFill}}`. This never touches `x`, `y`, `width`, `height`, or any other field.
- **Different fields:** If user B moved the shape (changed `x`, `y`) while user A changed `fill`, then A undoes: only `fill` reverts. B's move is preserved. No clobbering.
- **Same field:** If user B also changed `fill`, then A undoes: A's reverse overwrites B's `fill` value. This is accepted LWW behavior for the deployment context (2-5 people on a call, verbal coordination available).
- **Undo add (sends delete):** Deletes the shape even if others have modified it since. Correct ├втВмтАЭ A intended to remove the shape they created.
- **Undo delete (sends add):** Re-adds the shape with A's pre-delete snapshot. Changes made by others between the delete and undo are lost. Accepted for deployment context.
- **Op on non-existent shape:** Server silently ignores. Client's optimistic state self-corrects on reconnect.

### Persistence Boundary

**Persisted (PostgreSQL):**
- Users, canvases, canvas memberships, shapes (current state).

**Not persisted (ephemeral):**
- Cursor positions (server in-memory)
- Undo/redo stacks (client in-memory)
- Canvas `seq` counter (server in-memory, resets on restart ├втВмтАЭ clients get fresh `init` on reconnect)
- Operation history (not stored)
- Online presence (server in-memory)

### Database Schema

Exact DDL for `server/schema.sql`:

```sql
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgcrypto;

CREATE TABLE users (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    username    TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    email       TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    password_hash TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE canvases (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name        TEXT NOT NULL,
    owner_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE canvas_members (
    canvas_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    user_id     UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    PRIMARY KEY (canvas_id, user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE shapes (
    id           UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    canvas_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    type         TEXT NOT NULL,
    x            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    y            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    width        DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    height       DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    fill         TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
    stroke       TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '#000000',
    stroke_width DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 2,
    text         TEXT,
    font_size    DOUBLE PRECISION
);

CREATE INDEX idx_shapes_canvas ON shapes(canvas_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_canvas_members_user ON canvas_members(user_id);
```

DB uses `snake_case` columns. Wire format uses `camelCase`. Server maps: `stroke_width` ├втАатАЭ `strokeWidth`, `font_size` ├втАатАЭ `fontSize`, `owner_id` ├втАатАЭ `ownerId`, `created_at` ├втАатАЭ `createdAt`.

### Auth / Canvas / Invite API Surface

All HTTP routes under `/api`. All return JSON. Auth-protected routes require `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.

**Auth:**

| Method | Path | Body | Response | Errors |
|--------|------|------|----------|--------|
| POST | `/api/auth/signup` | `SignupRequest` | `AuthResponse` | 409 `username_taken`, `email_taken` |
| POST | `/api/auth/login` | `LoginRequest` | `AuthResponse` | 401 `invalid_credentials` |
| GET | `/api/auth/me` | ├втВмтАЭ | `AuthUser` | 401 `unauthorized` |

**Canvas:**

| Method | Path | Body | Response | Errors |
|--------|------|------|----------|--------|
| GET | `/api/canvases` | ├втВмтАЭ | `CanvasSummary[]` | 401 |
| POST | `/api/canvases` | `CreateCanvasRequest` | `CanvasSummary` | 401 |
| GET | `/api/canvases/:id` | ├втВмтАЭ | `CanvasDetail` | 401, 403 `not_a_member`, 404 `not_found` |
| POST | `/api/canvases/:id/invite` | `InviteRequest` | `{ ok: true }` | 401, 403 `not_a_member`, 404 `user_not_found` / `canvas_not_found` |

Canvas creator is automatically added to `canvas_members`. `GET /api/canvases` returns all canvases where user is a member.

---

## Ownership Map

| Module | Owner | Write Scope | Blocked By | Done When |
|--------|-------|-------------|------------|-----------|
| **infra-runtime** | session-1 | `server/schema.sql`, `server/app/db.py`, `server/app/main.py`, `server/app/deps.py`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `server/Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml` | contract | Schema applies, health check passes, `get_current_user` dep works, CORS configured |
| **backend-auth-canvas** | session-2 | `server/app/auth.py`, `server/app/canvas.py` | infra-runtime | All auth + canvas HTTP endpoints return correct shapes per contract |
| **backend-realtime-persistence** | session-3 | `server/app/ws.py` | infra-runtime | WS connects with auth, sends `init` with seq, echoes ops to ALL with seq+opId, per-canvas lock serializes ops, cursors + presence work |
| **frontend-whiteboard** | session-4 | `client/src/` (all files except `types.ts`) | contract | Auth flow, canvas list/create/invite, shape tools + interactions, undo/redo with opId matching, live cursors, state reloads on refresh |
| **validator** | session-5 | none (read-only) | all builders | PASS/FAIL report for all checks including type parity |

**Execution phases:**
```
Phase 1 (parallel):  infra-runtime  +  frontend-whiteboard
Phase 2 (parallel):  backend-auth-canvas  +  backend-realtime-persistence   ├втАа┬Р after infra only
Phase 3:             validator                                               ├втАа┬Р after all builders
Phase 4:             integration (wire routers into main.py, fix FAILs)
```

## Naming Rules

**Server (Python):**
- Files: `snake_case.py` in `server/app/`
- Functions: `snake_case` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `create_user`, `get_canvas`
- Route handlers: `snake_case` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `signup`, `login`, `list_canvases`
- Router exports: `auth_router = APIRouter()` in `auth.py`, `canvas_router = APIRouter()` in `canvas.py`
- WebSocket export: `async def websocket_endpoint(ws: WebSocket, ...)` in `ws.py`
- Dependencies: `get_current_user` in `deps.py`, returns `AuthUser` dict
- Imports: absolute from `app` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `from app.types import Shape`, `from app.deps import get_current_user`

**Client (TypeScript):**
- Files: `PascalCase.tsx` for components, `camelCase.ts` for non-components
- Functions/hooks: `camelCase` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `useAuth`, `useCanvas`, `useWebSocket`
- Components: `PascalCase` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `Canvas`, `Toolbar`, `LoginForm`
- Types: imported from `./types.ts`, never redefined locally
- Imports: relative paths with `.tsx`/`.ts` extensions (per tsconfig `allowImportingTsExtensions`)

**Wire format:**
- All JSON keys: `camelCase`
- Error responses: `{ "error": "snake_code" }` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `"invalid_credentials"`

- Verify user is a canvas member. Reject: close 4001 (auth) or 4003 (not member).
- Accept connection.

State (module-level):
- presence: dict[str, dict[str, {username, color, ws}]] ├втВмтАЭ canvasId ├втАатАЩ userId ├втАатАЩ info.
- seq_counters: dict[str, int] ├втВмтАЭ canvasId ├втАатАЩ current seq (starts 0, in-memory).
- canvas_locks: dict[str, asyncio.Lock] ├втВмтАЭ one lock per canvas for serialized op processing.

On connect:
- Assign cursor color from CURSOR_PALETTE (round-robin).
- Add to presence map.
- Send init: all shapes for canvas (SELECT from DB), online users, current seq.
- Broadcast join to other clients on the canvas.

On op message:
- Acquire canvas lock.
- Persist: add├втАатАЩINSERT (ignore if exists), update├втАатАЩUPDATE specific cols (ignore if missing),
  delete├втАатАЩDELETE (ignore if missing). Track whether DB was actually modified.
- If modified: increment seq, broadcast {type:"op", op, userId, seq, opId} to ALL clients including sender.
- If not modified (no-op): do not broadcast, do not increment seq.
- Release lock.

On cursor message:
- Broadcast to all OTHER clients on the canvas. Not persisted.

On disconnect:
- Remove from presence map.
- Broadcast leave to remaining clients.

## Backend-Realtime-Persistence Checks

### R1: WS Auth
Connects at `/ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`. Validates JWT and canvas membership. Rejects with close 4001 (auth) or 4003 (not member).

### R2: Init Message
On connect sends `{type:"init", shapes:[...], users:[...], seq:<int>}`. Shapes loaded from DB. Users from presence map. `seq` is current canvas counter.

### R3: Op Echo to ALL
On receiving a valid op: persists to DB, increments seq, broadcasts `{type:"op", op, userId, seq, opId}` to **ALL** clients including sender. NOT just others.

### R4: Op Serialization
Ops for a canvas are processed under a per-canvas `asyncio.Lock` (or equivalent). Seq is strictly monotonic per canvas.

### R5: No-Op Handling
If op target doesn't exist (update/delete on missing shape, add on existing id): no broadcast, no seq increment. Silent ignore.

### R6: Persistence
`add` ├втАатАЩ INSERT, `update` ├втАатАЩ UPDATE only specified columns, `delete` ├втАатАЩ DELETE. Parameterized queries, no raw string interpolation.

### R7: Cursor Broadcast
`cursor` messages broadcast to all OTHER clients on the canvas. Not persisted.

### R8: Presence Join/Leave
On connect: assigns color from `CURSOR_PALETTE`, adds to presence, broadcasts `join` to others. On disconnect: removes from presence, broadcasts `leave`.

### R9: Export
Exports `websocket_endpoint` function importable by main.py.

### R10: Ownership
All changes within `server/app/ws.py`. No other files touched.

ShapeType = Literal["rectangle", "ellipse", "line", "text"]


class _ShapeRequired(TypedDict):
    id: str
    type: ShapeType
    x: float
    y: float
    width: float
    height: float
    fill: str
    stroke: str
    strokeWidth: float


class Shape(_ShapeRequired, total=False):
    """Optional fields present only for type == 'text'."""
    text: str
    fontSize: float


# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Operations ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


class AddOp(TypedDict):
    kind: Literal["add"]
    shape: Shape


class UpdateOp(TypedDict):
    kind: Literal["update"]
    shapeId: str
    props: dict  # Partial<ShapeProps> ├втВмтАЭ validated at runtime


class DeleteOp(TypedDict):
    kind: Literal["delete"]
    shapeId: str


Operation = AddOp | UpdateOp | DeleteOp

# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм WebSocket Messages ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


class PresenceUser(TypedDict):
    userId: str
    username: str
    color: str


# Client ├втАатАЩ Server

class OpClientMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["op"]
    op: Operation
    opId: str  # client-generated UUIDv4, echoed by server


class CursorClientMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["cursor"]
    x: float
    y: float


ClientMessage = OpClientMsg | CursorClientMsg

# Server ├втАатАЩ Client


class InitServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["init"]
    shapes: list[Shape]
    users: list[PresenceUser]
    seq: int  # current canvas sequence counter


class OpServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["op"]
    op: Operation
    userId: str
    seq: int    # server-assigned, monotonically increasing per canvas
    opId: str   # echoed from client


class CursorServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["cursor"]
    userId: str
    username: str
    x: float
    y: float


class JoinServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["join"]
    user: PresenceUser


class LeaveServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["leave"]
    userId: str


class ErrorServerMsg(TypedDict):
    type: Literal["error"]
    message: str


ServerMessage = (
    InitServerMsg | OpServerMsg | CursorServerMsg

Output:
# AGENTS.md

Use this file for scoped handoffs and post-build review in this repository. Read `CLAUDE.md` first.

## Non-Negotiables

- Stay on the provided stack. Do not swap React, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, or Vite.
- Work on `main`. Commit early and often. Push directly to `main`. No PR workflow.
- Optimize for the actual deployment context: 2-5 users, same canvas, same call, simultaneous edits, 30-90 minute sessions, persistence across sessions.
- Correctness for required features matters more than polishing out-of-scope features.
- Keep collaborative text editing simple. Treat text edits as whole-object updates.
- Prefer simple, explicit conflict behavior over clever distributed systems work. Last-write-wins is acceptable here.

## Shared Review Priorities

Every builder and reviewer should care about these first:

1. Client and server agree on canvas object and websocket payload shapes.
2. Undo and redo semantics are internally consistent and do not corrupt persisted state.
3. Simultaneous edits to the same object have defined behavior.
4. Presence and cursor updates are ephemeral and do not pollute persisted board state.
5. Canvas state reloads correctly across sessions.
6. Authentication and canvas access are enforced on both HTTP and websocket paths.

## Canonical Ownership

If work is split, keep ownership boundaries clean.

### Agent: `infra-runtime`

- Scope:
  - `docker-compose.yml`
  - `client/Dockerfile`
  - `server/Dockerfile`
  - `client/vite.config.ts`
  - `SETUP.md`
- Responsibilities:
  - deterministic local startup
  - Docker networking fixes
  - health and readiness behavior
  - environment-variable wiring
- Must not own:
  - whiteboard domain logic
  - websocket protocol design
  - client canvas behavior

### Agent: `backend-domain`

- Scope:
  - `server/app/**`
  - `server/schema.sql`
- Responsibilities:
  - auth endpoints
  - canvas CRUD and invite flow
  - websocket protocol
  - persistence model
  - undo/redo persistence semantics
  - cursor broadcast and board event handling
- Must not own:
  - React component structure
  - Vite or Docker behavior unless blocked by backend bring-up

### Agent: `frontend-whiteboard`

- Scope:
  - `client/src/**`
  - client-only package updates if required
- Responsibilities:
  - auth screens and session state
  - canvas UI
  - shape creation and manipulation
  - remote cursor rendering
  - websocket client integration
  - undo and redo UX
- Must not own:
  - backend protocol changes without updating the shared contract first
  - Docker or DB changes unless explicitly reassigned

### Agent: `reviewer`

- Scope:
  - read-only across the whole repo
- Responsibilities:
  - code review after feature work
  - identify regressions, risks, missing validation, and protocol mismatches
  - verify required features against the take-home rubric
- Must not do:
  - opportunistic refactors
  - style-only feedback unless style causes defects
  - implementation work unless explicitly asked

## Handoff Requirements

Any handoff should include:

- exact write scope
- files changed
- public exports added or changed
- API routes added or changed
- websocket message types added or changed
- schema changes
- follow-up blockers
- what still needs validation

Use this format:

```md
## Handoff
- Scope:
- Files changed:
- Routes changed:
- Websocket messages changed:
- Schema changes:
- New env vars:
- Risks or blockers:
- Next validator checks:
```

## Builder Prompt Template

```text
Read CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md first.
Own only [write-scope].
Implement [task].
Preserve the provided stack and current repo conventions.
Do not change shapes, websocket payloads, or persistence semantics outside your scope without calling it out explicitly.
At the end, provide a handoff using the AGENTS.md format.
```

## Reviewer Prompt Template

```text
Read CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md first.
Review the finished implementation in a code-review mindset.
Prioritize bugs, robustness gaps, behavioral regressions, protocol mismatches, missing validation, and missing tests.
Focus on the actual take-home requirements and the synchronous-collaboration deployment context.
Do not fix code. Report findings first, ordered by severity, with file references.
```

## Whiteboard-Specific Review Checklist

The reviewer should explicitly check:

- accounts: signup, login, logout actually work
- canvas access: create, open, invite work end to end
- persistence: reloading a canvas restores the latest state
- shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, and text all round-trip correctly
- manipulation: select, move, resize, recolor, delete work locally and remotely
- collaboration: two users see each other's edits promptly
- cursor presence: cursors render for other users and disappear on disconnect
- websocket auth: unauthorized users cannot attach to arbitrary canvases
- undo and redo: behavior is predictable under concurrent edits
- conflict handling: same-object edits do not crash or corrupt board state
- reconnect behavior: stale or disconnected clients do not leave broken presence state behind

## Recommended Review Order

1. `server/schema.sql`
2. `server/app/**` auth, canvas, invite, websocket, persistence
3. `client/src/**` state model and websocket integration
4. cross-check shared payload shapes between client and server
5. Docker and local run flow

## Stop Conditions

Pause and escalate instead of pushing ahead when:

- client and server payload shapes diverge
- undo and redo behavior is undefined under concurrent edits
- websocket messages are added ad hoc without a stable envelope
- auth is implemented for HTTP but not websocket access
- canvas persistence and live collaboration use different shape models

## Notes For This Repo

- The current scaffold is minimal. Expect most required behavior to be built from scratch.
- `server/schema.sql` is the canonical place for DDL.
- `asyncpg` uses `$1`, `$2` placeholders. Do not use string-formatted SQL.
- The frontend should talk through `/api/*` and `/ws`, not hardcoded server ports.
- If a contract file is introduced later, treat it as architect-owned and keep reviewers focused on contract compliance.

How do we use batch on Claude code.

4:56 PM

Assembled sufficient knowledge to address batch functionality
Assembled sufficient knowledge to address batch functionality
The /batch command in Claude Code is a slash command for parallelizing large-scale codebase changes. Here's how it works:

Basic usage тАФ just type /batch followed by a natural language description of what you want:

/batch migrate from React to Vue
/batch add type annotations to all untyped functions
/batch replace all uses of lodash with native equivalents
How it works under the hood:

When executed, an internal orchestrator agent launches and processes the work in three phases. 
SmartScope
 It explores your codebase, breaks your task into discrete units тАФ one per file or one per defined scope тАФ and runs them concurrently using multiple Claude instances. 
MindStudio
 Typically it decomposes into 5тАУ30 independent units. Each worker automatically runs /simplify on its own changes before creating PRs. 
Pasquale Pillitteri

When to use it:

Library/framework migrations
Dependency swaps
Bulk test generation
Renaming conventions across many files
Adding error handling project-wide
When NOT to use it:

Tightly coupled changes where file A depends on file B's new output aren't independent units. Use standard Claude Code sessions or agent teams for coordinated multi-agent work instead. 
Claude Fast

Tips for good results:

Be specific about scope тАФ "all TypeScript files in /src/api" beats "the API folder." Define the transformation precisely so Claude can apply it consistently. 
MindStudio

Requirements: Claude Code v2.1.63 or higher. Check with claude --version and update with claude update if needed. 
Pasquale Pillitteri

Note this is different from the Batch API (for sending bulk API requests at 50% cost) and from headless mode (claude -p) which is for CI/CD integration. /batch is specifically for interactive parallel codebase migrations.
