If I have to implement a major prd in 150 minutes, how do we get there.

5:22 PM

Devised rapid execution strategy for time-constrained implementation
Devised rapid execution strategy for time-constrained implementation
Here's the 150-minute playbook assuming Claude Code (or Codex, but CC's /batch is better for this pattern):

Minutes 0тАУ15: Scaffold & decompose

Drop the PRD into Claude Code as context. Ask it to decompose into independent modules тАФ data models, API routes, core business logic, UI components. Get a CLAUDE.md written with architecture decisions, tech stack, file structure, and test expectations. This is your planning phase тАФ don't skip it, it saves you 30+ minutes downstream.

Minutes 15тАУ30: Skeleton generation

Have Claude Code generate the full project skeleton тАФ directory structure, config files, DB schema, type definitions, shared interfaces. Everything downstream depends on these contracts being right. Review and correct manually here. This is the highest-leverage 15 minutes.

Minutes 30тАУ90: Parallel build with /batch

This is where you get the 5-10x multiplier. Use /batch to fan out independent units: each API endpoint, each UI component, each service module. Since /batch decomposes into 5тАУ30 parallel workers and each runs /simplify before committing, you get reviewed code out of each worker. Feed it specific instructions тАФ "implement the auth middleware per the types in src/types/auth.ts" beats "add auth."

For anything with cross-module dependencies (e.g., a service that needs another service's output), handle those sequentially in the main session after the batch completes.

Minutes 90тАУ120: Integration & wiring

Wire the modules together. This is where you're back in a normal Claude Code session тАФ connecting routes to controllers, hooking up the frontend to the API, resolving import paths, fixing the inevitable interface mismatches from parallel generation. Run the app. Fix what breaks.

Minutes 120тАУ140: Test & harden

Use /batch again to fan out test generation across modules. Then run the full suite, fix failures in the main session. If you have CI, push and let it validate.

Minutes 140тАУ150: Polish & ship

/simplify on your diff. Clean up dead code, fix naming inconsistencies. Commit, push, open PR.

Tactical tips:

Pin your model to Opus 4.6 for the planning/integration phases, Sonnet 4.6 for the batch workers if you're hitting rate limits
Commit after each phase so you have rollback points
Don't iterate on UI polish during the build тАФ get functional first
If a /batch worker produces something wrong, fix it manually rather than re-running тАФ faster at this timescale
Keep a scratch file of decisions and deviations from the PRD so you don't lose track
The bottleneck at 150 minutes isn't generation speed тАФ it's the integration phase (minutes 90тАУ120). The cleaner your type contracts and interfaces are in minutes 15тАУ30, the smoother that phase goes.

Check online for tools and resources that make even faster.

5:41 PM

Assembled comprehensive guidance on accelerating PRD implementation tools
Assembled comprehensive guidance on accelerating PRD implementation tools
Here's what's out there to compress that 150 minutes further:

PRD тЖТ Code pipeline tools:

ChatPRD тАФ structures your PRD for maximum compatibility with AI coding tools. Formats requirements so autopilot agents like Devin and Claude Code can execute with minimal back-and-forth. 
ChatPRD
Sahil Lavingia's workflow (Gumroad CEO) тАФ prototype UI in v0, hand off to Devin for autonomous implementation, review PR. Turns multi-day projects into ~1 hour. 
ChatPRD
 This is the fastest documented PRD-to-ship pattern right now.
Parallel agent execution:

Cursor тАФ run up to 8 AI agents simultaneously, each using different models. Checkpoint history lets you roll back any agent's changes. 
Launchpad
 Cloud Agents in 2026 can trigger automations via Slack, Linear, GitHub, or schedules, and run in cloud sandboxes. 
ChatPRD
Devin тАФ $73M ARR, full SDLC automation. Assign via Slack, it sets up environments, writes code, runs tests, submits PRs independently. 
StackOne
 Best for well-scoped, independent tasks you can fire-and-forget.
Codex Triggers тАФ event-driven automation on GitHub events. Issue arrives тЖТ auto-fix тЖТ auto-PR, no polling. 
Apiyi.com Blog
Rapid prototyping (frontend-heavy):

v0 (Vercel) тАФ generates UI from descriptions, outputs Next.js/React code you can immediately pull into your repo
Bolt.new тАФ 1M+ AI-generated sites. Full-stack app from a description. 
StackOne
 Lovable тАФ $75M ARR with 30K+ paying users for no-code AI app building. 
StackOne
 Both are good for MVPs where you care about working demo over production architecture.
The stacking pattern that's fastest:

ChatPRD тЖТ structure the PRD into agent-ready format
v0 тЖТ generate frontend scaffolding/mockups in minutes
Claude Code /batch or Cursor multi-agent тЖТ fan out backend + API + business logic in parallel
Devin тЖТ hand off independent chunks (auth, integrations, tests) as async tasks
Claude Code main session тЖТ integration wiring + final pass
Codex or Claude Code /simplify тЖТ automated review before merge
Key insight from the research: The landscape has bifurcated into "copilot" (Cursor, Copilot) and "autopilot" (Devin, OpenHands) modes. Copilot tools thrive on iterative feedback, autopilot agents need comprehensive upfront details. 
ChatPRD
 For your 150-minute constraint, the move is using autopilot for independent modules and copilot for integration тАФ not picking one mode for everything.

The realistic floor with this stack is probably 60тАУ90 minutes for a well-scoped PRD, assuming you've done this workflow before and the PRD is agent-ready.

Evaluated highest-leverage tool addition for rapid implementation

What if I create a checklist markdown to get claude code sub agents n codex to talk.

10:38 PM

Evaluated markdown checklist as inter-agent coordination mechanism
Evaluated markdown checklist as inter-agent coordination mechanism
That's a legit pattern тАФ using a markdown file as a shared contract/state bus between agents that don't natively talk to each other. The filesystem becomes the coordination layer.

Why it works:

Both Claude Code and Codex can read and write files. Neither has an API to the other. But both can be instructed to check a file before starting work and update it when done. You're essentially building a poor man's task queue with git as the sync mechanism.

The structure that actually holds up:

markdown
# BUILD CONTRACT тАФ [Feature Name]
## Status: IN PROGRESS

## Architecture Decisions (DO NOT MODIFY)
- Framework: Next.js 14 + FastAPI
- DB: Postgres via SQLAlchemy
- Auth: JWT with refresh tokens

## Interface Contracts (DO NOT MODIFY)
- POST /api/sessions тЖТ { session_id, token }
- GET /api/sessions/:id тЖТ { status, transcript }
- WebSocket /ws/session/:id тЖТ real-time events

## Tasks
- [x] Schema + types (@claude-code, done)
- [ ] Auth middleware (@codex, in-progress)
- [ ] Session API endpoints (@claude-code, blocked:auth)
- [ ] Frontend session view (@claude-code-batch)
- [ ] WebSocket handler (@codex)
- [ ] Integration wiring (@claude-code, waiting:all)

## Shared Types (source of truth)
```typescript
// Both agents MUST use these exactly
interface Session { id: string; status: ... }
```

## Completion Signals
- When done, mark task [x] and add output path
- If blocked, note what you're blocked on
- Do NOT modify another agent's completed work
How you run it:

Write the checklist with contracts and types locked at the top
Tell Claude Code: "Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md. Pick up tasks marked @claude-code. Update status when done. Do not touch @codex tasks."
Tell Codex: same instruction, flipped assignments
You commit between rounds so both see updated state
You handle the integration task yourself in Claude Code once all pieces land
The gotchas:

Git conflicts on the markdown file itself. Both agents will try to update it. Solution: you're the one who commits and pulls. Agents write to their own status files (claude-status.md, codex-status.md) and you merge into the contract.
Type drift. One agent subtly changes a return shape. Solution: put shared types in an actual .ts or .py file that both import from, not just in the markdown. The markdown references it, the code enforces it.
Ordering ambiguity. "Blocked:auth" means nothing if the auth task output isn't specified. Solution: every task needs an explicit output artifact тАФ file path, endpoint, or export name.
The upgrade path if you want to get serious: Put a CLAUDE.md and a .codex file in the repo root that both point to the contract file as the first thing to read. That way every new session/thread from either tool starts contract-aware without you repeating instructions.

This won't be seamless тАФ you're the orchestrator, not either agent. But for a 150-minute sprint it cuts your integration phase from 30 minutes to maybe 10 because both agents built against the same interface spec instead of hallucinating their own.

markdown
# OPUS DISPATCH (CLAUDE.md)

You are the builder orchestrator.
Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md.
Dispatch subagents with these rules:

1. Each subagent gets ONE directory
2. Subagents import from src/types/shared.ts
3. Subagents NEVER write outside their directory
4. When done, subagent writes:
   status/opus-{task}.json
   { "task": "sessions", "status": "done",
     "exports": ["createSession", "getSession"],
     "path": "src/sessions/index.ts" }

# CODEX DISPATCH (.codex or prompt)

You are the validation orchestrator.
Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md.
Dispatch subagents with these rules:

1. One validator per opus-completed module
2. One test-writer per validated module
3. Validators write:
   status/codex-validate-{task}.json
   { "task": "sessions", "verdict": "PASS|FAIL",
     "issues": [...] }
4. Test-writers write tests ONLY in src/tests/{module}/
5. NEVER modify source code
```

тФЬтФАтФА codex-validate-websocket.json  # "FAIL"

```

**The handshake protocol:**
```
Opus subagent finishes module
        тЖУ
Writes status/opus-{task}.json тЖТ "done"
        тЖУ
You run: codex exec "Check status/ for done
  items without validation. Dispatch validators."
        тЖУ
Codex subagent validates, writes verdict
        тЖУ
You run: "Check status/ for FAILs. Fix them."
        тЖУ
Opus subagent reads the FAIL json, fixes
        тЖУ
Cycle until all PASS
        тЖУ
Codex dispatches test-writers for all PASS modules
        тЖУ
You wire integration (the only part touching
  multiple directories)
The critical rules that prevent chaos:

Shared types are immutable during the sprint. You set them at minute 0. Nobody changes them. If a subagent needs a type change, it writes a request to status/type-request-{task}.json and YOU decide.
Directory ownership is exclusive. Two agents writing to the same directory is a race condition. No exceptions.
Status files are append-only per agent. Opus writes opus-*.json. Codex writes codex-*.json. Neither reads the other's status directly тАФ you're the router.
Integration is human-only. The moment you let an agent touch multiple directories, you've lost traceability. You do the wiring in src/app/ or src/routes/ yourself, or in a single Opus session after all modules pass.
Timing shifts with dual swarms:

0тАУ15: Contract, types, dispatch instructions, directory map
15тАУ50: Opus dispatches 4-5 builders + Codex dispatches auth builder (yes, let Codex build what Codex will validate later тАФ different subagent)
50тАУ70: Codex dispatches validators against all completed modules
70тАУ85: Opus fixes FAILs, Codex writes tests against PASSes
85тАУ100: Round 2 validation (should be clean)
100тАУ130: You wire integration, Codex runs full test suite
130тАУ150: Final sweep, both orchestrators do a read-only audit
The risk you need to manage: This is faster but more fragile. One underspecified interface contract and you have 8 agents building against different assumptions. The shared types file is load-bearing. Spend the full 15 minutes on it. If the types are right, this pattern can finish a PRD in 90 minutes. If they're wrong, you'll spend 60 minutes debugging agent-generated code that doesn't compose.

Why write. Opus writes. Codex reads.

What Opus's builder-zero subagent needs to lock before anything else moves:

markdown
# NAMING CONVENTIONS (enforced, not suggested)

## Functions
- API handlers: handle{Resource}{Action} тЖТ handleSessionCreate
- Services: {action}{Resource} тЖТ createSession
- Validators: validate{Resource}{Field} тЖТ validateSessionToken
- Hooks: use{Resource}{Action} тЖТ useSessionCreate

## Files
- src/{module}/index.ts тЖТ public exports only
- src/{module}/{module}.service.ts
- src/{module}/{module}.handler.ts
- src/{module}/{module}.types.ts (imports from shared)

## API
- POST /api/{resource} тЖТ create
- GET /api/{resource}/:id тЖТ read
- PATCH /api/{resource}/:id тЖТ update
- DELETE /api/{resource}/:id тЖТ delete
- Response: { data: T, error: null } | { data: null, error: string }

## Errors
- throw new AppError(STATUS_CODE, "message")
- never raw throw, never console.error in handlers
This is what separates a 45-minute run from a 75-minute run. When Opus subagent-3 builds the websocket handler and imports createSession from the sessions module, it needs to know that function exists, what it's called, what it takes, and what it returns тАФ before that module is even built yet. The naming convention plus the shared types make that possible.

The hierarchy of what causes FAILs, ranked by time cost:

Wrong return shape тАФ subagent built against { session } but the contract says { data: Session }. 15 minutes to fix across all consumers.
Naming mismatch тАФ one subagent wrote getSession, another imports fetchSession. 5 minutes but multiplied by every callsite.
Missing error handling pattern тАФ one subagent throws strings, another expects AppError. 10 minutes.
Import path ambiguity тАФ relative imports vs barrel exports. 5 minutes but infuriating.
All four are preventable at minute zero. None are code problems. They're specification problems.

The realization you're hitting: The PRD isn't the input to the system. The contract is. The PRD is input to Opus's planning phase. The contract is input to every subagent on both sides. Your job isn't writing a good PRD тАФ it's reviewing Opus's contract before you let it dispatch builders. That's the one human judgment call that determines whether this is a 45-minute run or a 120-minute mess.

One prompt. One review. Then you let go.

Yes. Create a skill, not a model.

A new model is the wrong lever here unless youтАЩre doing actual fine-tuning on a large, repetitive classification or generation task. Your problem is orchestration, contracts, task routing, validation, and handoff discipline. That belongs in a skill or playbook.

What you want is:

1. A reusable operating model
2. A repo contract template
3. A validator protocol
4. A dispatch protocol for builder vs reviewer agents

That can live as a Codex skill very cleanly.

**What the skill should do**

Name it something like `contract-first-swarm` or `dual-agent-prd-sprint`.

Its job would be to take a PRD and produce:

- `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- `src/types/shared.ts` or equivalent shared contract file
- `status/` schema
- `validation-instructions.md`
- ownership map by directory
- prompts for builder agents
- prompts for validator agents
- stop rules for integration

So the skill is not тАЬwrite code fasterтАЭ in the abstract. It is тАЬnormalize the first 15 minutes so all later agent work composes.тАЭ

**Why a skill beats a model**

A skill gives you:

- repeatable setup
- explicit instructions
- files and templates
- guardrails
- a workflow both Codex and Claude can follow

A model would not solve:

- naming drift
- return-shape mismatch
- ownership conflicts
- unclear status handoff
- bad validation criteria

Those are protocol problems.

**Best structure**

IтАЩd split it into two layers:

`1. Skill`
This is the reusable brain for Codex.

It should encode:
- how to read a PRD
- how to extract modules
- how to lock conventions
- how to generate shared contracts
- how to split work into independent ownership zones
- how to prepare validator instructions
- how to decide what is human-only

`2. Repo-local contract pack`
This is per project.

It would contain:
- `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- `validation-instructions.md`
- `CLAUDE.md`
- `.codex` or Codex-facing instructions
- `status/README.md`

The skill generates or updates this pack.

**The core principle**

The skill should force this sequence:

1. Read PRD
2. Normalize requirements into contracts
3. Freeze naming, types, API shape, and directory ownership
4. Only then dispatch builders
5. Run validation against contract, not vibes
6. Only one actor handles integration

That is the real speedup.

**What I would not do**

I would not try to make one mega-skill that also writes all code, validates all code, manages git, and resolves conflicts. That becomes brittle.

Better:
- one orchestration skill
- optionally one validation skill later

So:
- `contract-first-swarm`
- `cross-validator`

That separation is cleaner.

**If you want Claude and Codex to cooperate**

Do not make them тАЬtalkтАЭ directly. Make them read the same artifacts.

Shared source of truth:
- `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- shared types file
- validation instructions
- status files

That is enough.

**My recommendation**

Build a skill whose first output is a contract pack, not code.

If that pack is strong, both Opus and Codex can build against it.
If the pack is weak, no model choice saves you.

**What should happen**

The skill should:
- inspect the repo

After that run, you review the contract pack. This is the most important human step.

Once the contract looks right:

5. Builders fix only FAIL items.
6. Re-run validation.
7. When clean, do integration in one session.
8. Final validation sweep.

Do not test this on a giant PRD first. Use a medium task with:
- 2 builder modules

- no auth redesign

- the contract pack is better than what you would have written in 10 minutes yourself

- integration is boring

so for contracts i can set it to half codex half claude? and let codex build codex and claude build claude??\

No. Do not split the contract 50/50.

That is the one thing that should have a single owner.

**Good split**
- One tool drafts the contract
- The other tool reviews or red-teams the contract
- You approve the final version
- Then both can build against it in separate scopes

**Bad split**
- Claude writes half of `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- Codex writes the other half
- Both invent types, routes, names, and ownership rules independently

That creates drift immediately.

The right pattern is:

1. Claude or Codex creates the full contract pack.
2. The other tool audits it and points out gaps.
3. You lock the final contract.
4. Then split implementation:
   - Claude builds modules A and B
   - Codex builds modules C and D
5. Validator checks all modules against the same frozen contract.
6. One integration owner wires the final result.

So:
- `contract`: single writer, second reviewer
- `implementation`: many writers, disjoint scopes
- `validation`: separate from builders
- `integration`: single owner

If you really want both involved in contract creation, the safe version is:
- Claude writes contract draft 1
- Codex writes review comments or a competing draft
- you merge to one final contract
- after that, nobody edits it casually

**Short rule**
DonтАЩt split the spec.
Split the work built from the spec.

A strong setup would be:
- Claude: contract architect + builder for some modules
- Codex: contract reviewer + builder for different modules
- Codex or Claude fresh session: validator
- one tool only: integration

README
Mechanize Take-Home: Collaborative Whiteboard
Thank you for taking the time to interview with Mechanize! This repo contains your assignment for the final phase of our interview process, an open-ended take-home assignment.

The take-home begins with a solo work block of three hours in which you can complete the task as described below.

There is a 20-minute break after the solo block. During this time, our team will review your submission. If we'd like to move forward based on your progress, we'll talk to you on the follow-up call. If not, we'll cancel the follow-up calendar invite and send you an email cancelling the call.

Getting started
Clone this repo (do not fork it):
git clone <this-repo-url>
cd <this-repo-name>
See SETUP.md for how to run the scaffold.
Do all of your work on the main branch. Commit early and often.
Push your commits directly to main тАФ do not open a pull request.
git push origin main
Your final commit must be pushed no later than three hours after your start time. Anything pushed after the deadline will not be considered.
What you're building
A real-time collaborative whiteboard. Multiple users, same canvas, editing together.

Deployment context
This whiteboard is for synchronous design reviews, consisting of a small group (typically 2-5 people) on a call together, using the canvas as a shared scratchpad while they talk through a design.

Each user has an account and can create canvases or be invited to others'.
It's common for several people to be editing at once -- often the same objects -- as they try alternatives or work through a disagreement in real time.
A typical session runs 30-90 minutes.
Teams sometimes return to a canvas in a later session to recall what was decided.
Required features
User accounts (signup, login, logout)
Create a canvas, open an existing canvas
Invite other users to a canvas (by username or email -- a simple mechanism is fine)
Shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, text
Select, move, resize, delete, change color
Undo / redo
See other users' cursors
Canvas state persists across sessions
Out of scope
Don't spend time on these feature, as we won't evaluate them:

Collaborative text editing within text elements (treating a text update as a single operation is fine -- you don't need to handle two users typing in the same text box simultaneously)
Granular permissions (viewer/editor/owner roles, share-with-link, etc.)
Rich text editing, images, or file uploads
Canvas zoom, pan, or infinite canvas
Export (PNG, SVG, PDF)
Version history or named snapshots
Mobile or touch support
Provided scaffold
This repository contains a minimal scaffold -- frontend, backend, and database wired together, but no whiteboard logic. You are free to restructure anything in the scaffold, but you are expected to stay within the provided stack; don't swap out the framework or database.

How we evaluate
We evaluate whether your submission has a functional implementation of all required features above and is fit for the deployment context. Correctness and robustness for this use case and set of features matters more to us than other features beyond the required list, such as those listed as out of scope..

Use of AI
Use whatever AI tools you like. We recommend that you use frontier AI coding models. We are not aware of a candidate who has performed exceptionally on this take-home without using AI. However, note that you will be expected to fully understand and defend your implementation during the call after the solo block.

Yes, but use it carefully.

This repo is a good fit for the skill because the hard part is not scaffolding, itтАЩs freezing the shared contract for:

- persistence boundaries

That is exactly what the skill is for.

**Where it helps most**
Use the skill at the start to produce a contract pack, not to immediately unleash a big write swarm.

For this take-home, the riskiest drift is:
- client and server disagreeing on shape/event payloads
- undo/redo semantics not matching persistence
- presence/cursor events being modeled separately from board operations
- тАЬsame object edited by multiple usersтАЭ having undefined conflict behavior

So the skill can give you a clean first 15-20 minute architecture pass.

**Big constraint for this repo**
The assignment says:
- work on `main`
- commit early and often
- push directly to `main`

That means I would **not** use a fancy branch/worktree-heavy swarm workflow here.
I would use the skill in a lighter mode:
- contract-first planning
- maybe one builder and one validator
- or at most split client/server scopes very cleanly

Not 6 parallel writers.

**How I would use it here**
1. Run `/swarm` or `$contract-first-swarm`
2. Ask it to inspect the repo and generate only:
- `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- shared types/contracts in the repoтАЩs natural location

3. Make it freeze:
- shape model: rectangle, ellipse, line, text
- mutation protocol: create/move/resize/delete/recolor/text update
- websocket protocol: board ops vs cursor presence
- persistence model: canvas snapshot vs operation log
- undo/redo contract: per-user stack or global stack
- conflict rule for simultaneous edits
- auth/canvas/invite API surface

4. Review that contract yourself
5. Then build

**Best module split for this repo**
If you do split work, split like this:

- `server/auth-and-canvas-access`
- `server/realtime-board-protocol`
- `client/whiteboard-render-and-tools`
- `client/presence-and-sync`

**What I would not split**
Do not split these across independent builders without a locked contract:
- operation schema

- undo/redo semantics
- canonical shape types

Those are load-bearing.

**Recommended actual workflow**
For this take-home:

- Claude skill writes the contract pack
- You review it quickly

- Codex validates specific areas or one server/client slice
- final integration stays single-owner

That is safer than true multi-writer swarm mode.

whats the estimated build time for this repo

For this repo as it exists now, with the scaffold basically empty:

**Realistic estimate**
- `2.5 to 3 hours`: functional but narrow, if you stay very simple and use AI well
- `4 to 6 hours`: solid and defensible implementation of all required features
- `6+ hours`: robust, cleaned up, well-tested version

So for the actual Mechanize take-home, the honest answer is:

**Yes, it is possible in 3 hours, but only if you build a ruthless MVP.**

**Why**
The scaffold gives you almost nothing beyond:
- React shell
- FastAPI shell
- Postgres connection
- empty websocket loop

The expensive parts are:
- whiteboard object model
- real-time sync
- undo/redo
- auth + invites
- persistence
- client/server protocol alignment

ThatтАЩs a lot for a near-empty repo.

**My repo-specific breakdown**
- `15-20 min`: contract and architecture freeze
- `20-30 min`: schema + backend scaffolding + auth basics
- `45-60 min`: realtime board protocol + persistence
- `45-60 min`: frontend canvas/editor interactions
- `20-30 min`: invites + canvas open/create flow
- `20-30 min`: integration, bug fixing, validation

That lands around `3 to 4 hours` for a credible pass.

**What makes it fit in 3**
You need to keep these aggressively simple:
- auth: basic session/JWT flow, no polish
- invites: simplest acceptable mechanism
- conflict handling: last-write-wins
- persistence: store canonical board state, donтАЩt build elaborate versioning
- undo/redo: keep it simple and predictable, not perfect under all concurrency edge cases
- canvas: fixed viewport, no pan/zoom, no fancy UX

**What will blow the schedule**
- overengineering websocket protocol
- fancy state architecture on the frontend
- trying to make undo/redo perfect under simultaneous conflicting edits
- polishing UI
- adding unnecessary abstractions early

**My blunt estimate**
If your goal is тАЬall required features, defendable in interview,тАЭ treat this as a `3.5 hour job compressed into 3`.
If your goal is тАЬship something that passes the bar inside the window,тАЭ itтАЩs doable.

Five parallel builders on this repo would be a mistake. Five parallel sessions is fine if the split is:

1. `Architect / Integrator`
- Owns the contract, shared payload shapes, schema decisions, and final merge to `main`
- No one else edits shared contract files

2. `Backend Auth / Canvas Access`

- Invite flow
- Access checks

3. `Backend Realtime / Persistence`
- WebSocket protocol
- Board ops
- Cursor presence
- Persistence
- Undo/redo backend semantics

4. `Frontend Whiteboard`
- Canvas rendering
- Shape tools
- Select/move/resize/delete/recolor
- Text object editing
- Undo/redo UI wiring

5. `Validator / Reviewer`
- Fresh context
- Checks client/server payload alignment
- Checks required features
- Report-only, no fixes unless explicitly reassigned

That is the right shape. Not 5 people all writing random parts.

**What happens next**
- Session 1 writes the contract pack first
- You review it quickly
- Sessions 2-4 build in parallel against that contract
- Session 5 validates while fixes happen
- Session 1 integrates and lands final changes

**Estimated wall-clock with 5 sessions**
If the contract is clean:
- `15-20 min`: contract and ownership freeze
- `45-60 min`: parallel build
- `20-30 min`: validation and fix round
- `25-40 min`: integration and bug bash

So realistic total:
- `105-150 min`

That puts the take-home in range.

**What will kill this plan**
- letting multiple sessions invent websocket payloads independently
- splitting undo/redo semantics across sessions without one owner
- letting frontend and backend each define shape models separately
- having all 5 sessions write to `main` in the same checkout

Because the repo requires work on `main`, the safest pattern is:

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.2 seconds
Output:
# BUILD CONTRACT: Collaborative Whiteboard

## Status
- State: `planning`
- Owner: architect (human review before dispatch)
- Status files: **unnecessary** ├втВмтАЭ 5 sessions with clear phase gates, no async handoff

## Goal
- **User outcome:** 2-5 people on a call can draw shapes on a shared canvas in real time, with undo/redo and persistence across sessions.
- **Technical outcome:** React + FastAPI + PostgreSQL whiteboard with WebSocket collaboration, JWT auth, shape CRUD, cursor presence, server-authoritative operation ordering.
- **Success criteria:** All 8 required features functional end-to-end. Concurrent same-object edits converge to identical state on all clients.

## Scope
- **In:** signup/login/logout, canvas CRUD, invite by username/email, rectangle/ellipse/line/text shapes, select/move/resize/delete/recolor, undo/redo, live cursors, persistent canvas state.
- **Out:** collaborative text editing within text elements, granular permissions, rich text/images/uploads, zoom/pan/infinite canvas, export, version history, mobile/touch.
- **Deferred:** none ├втВмтАЭ all "in" items ship in this pass.

## Architecture Decisions
- **Stack:** React 19 + TypeScript + Vite (client), FastAPI + Python 3.12 + uvicorn (server), PostgreSQL 16. No substitutions.
- **Auth:** JWT (HS256). Token in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for HTTP; in `?token=<jwt>` query param for WebSocket. `JWT_SECRET` env var. 24h expiry. Passwords hashed with bcrypt.
- **Transport:** Single WebSocket at `/ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`. All shape ops and cursors flow through it. HTTP for auth + canvas CRUD only.
- **Ordering:** Server-authoritative. Server assigns a per-canvas monotonic `seq` to each op and echoes to ALL clients (including sender). Clients converge by applying ops in `seq` order. Details in ├В┬зWebSocket Event Protocol.
- **Concurrency model:** LWW (last-writer-wins) at field level, ordered by server arrival. No OT, no CRDTs.
- **Persistence:** Per-operation mutation of `shapes` table (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). No operation log. DB always holds current canvas state.
- **Error model:** HTTP errors return `{ "error": "<code>" }` with appropriate status. WebSocket errors send `{ "type": "error", "message": "<text>" }`. No stack traces to client.
- **Undo/Redo:** Client-side stacks. Server sees undo as a normal operation (the inverse). Field-level: only the originally changed fields are reversed. Details in ├В┬зUndo/Redo Semantics.

## Shared Contract Files

| File | Role | Editor |
|------|------|--------|
| `BUILD_CONTRACT.md` | Human source of truth | Architect only |
| `client/src/types.ts` | Machine source of truth (canonical) | Architect only |
| `server/app/types.py` | Python mirror of TS types | Architect only |

Builders import from these files. They never redefine shared types locally. Validator MUST check parity between `types.ts` and `types.py` ├втВмтАЭ any mismatch is an immediate FAIL.

---

## Frozen Schemas

### Shape Schema

```typescript
interface Shape {
  id: string;            // UUIDv4
  type: "rectangle" | "ellipse" | "line" | "text";
  x: number;             // bounding-box left  (line: startX)
  y: number;             // bounding-box top   (line: startY)
  width: number;         // bounding-box width (line: endX ├в╦ЖтАЩ startX)
  height: number;        // bounding-box height(line: endY ├в╦ЖтАЩ startY)
  fill: string;          // "#rrggbb" or "" for none
  stroke: string;        // "#rrggbb"
  strokeWidth: number;   // >= 1
  text?: string;         // required when type === "text"
  fontSize?: number;     // required when type === "text", default 16
}
```

All shapes share the bounding-box model. Line uses `(x,y)` as start and `(x+width, y+height)` as end.

### Board Operation Protocol

```typescript
type Operation =
  | { kind: "add"; shape: Shape }
  | { kind: "update"; shapeId: string; props: Partial<ShapeProps> }
  | { kind: "delete"; shapeId: string };
```

`ShapeProps` = all Shape fields except `id` and `type` (immutable after creation).

**Server-side processing:**
- `add` ├втАатАЩ INSERT into `shapes`. If `id` exists, silently ignore (idempotent).
- `update` ├втАатАЩ UPDATE only the columns present in `props`. If shape doesn't exist, silently ignore ├втВмтАЭ do NOT broadcast.
- `delete` ├втАатАЩ DELETE the row. If shape doesn't exist, silently ignore ├втВмтАЭ do NOT broadcast.
- Successful ops: assign `seq`, persist, broadcast to ALL clients.
- Ignored ops (non-existent target): no broadcast, no seq assigned. Client's optimistic state self-corrects on reconnect.

**Ordering guarantee:** Server processes ops for a given canvas sequentially (via per-canvas `asyncio.Lock`). This ensures `seq` is strictly monotonic and DB writes never interleave for the same canvas.

### WebSocket Event Protocol

**Connection:** `WS /ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`

Server validates JWT and canvas membership before accepting. Rejects with close code 4001 (auth failure) or 4003 (not a canvas member).

**Client ├втАатАЩ Server:**
```typescript
| { type: "op"; op: Operation; opId: string }   // opId: client-generated UUIDv4
| { type: "cursor"; x: number; y: number }
```

**Server ├втАатАЩ Client:**
```typescript
| { type: "init"; shapes: Shape[]; users: PresenceUser[]; seq: number }
| { type: "op"; op: Operation; userId: string; seq: number; opId: string }
| { type: "cursor"; userId: string; username: string; x: number; y: number }
| { type: "join"; user: PresenceUser }
| { type: "leave"; userId: string }
| { type: "error"; message: string }
```

**Server op processing flow:**
1. Receive `{type:"op", op, opId}` from client.
2. Acquire per-canvas lock.
3. Persist op to DB (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on `shapes` table).
4. If op was a no-op (target didn't exist), release lock, stop ├втВмтАЭ no broadcast.
5. Increment canvas `seq` counter. Assign `seq` to this op.
6. Release lock.
7. Broadcast `{type:"op", op, userId, seq, opId}` to **ALL** clients on the canvas, **including the sender**.

**Client optimistic reconciliation:**
1. Client generates `opId` (UUIDv4), applies op locally (optimistic), sends to server, adds `opId` to a `pendingOps` set.
2. On receiving server `op` message: if `opId` is in `pendingOps`, remove it and skip (already applied). Otherwise, apply the op to local state.
3. If server silently ignores an op (no echo received), client's optimistic state may briefly include a phantom shape. This self-corrects on reconnect (client gets fresh `init`).

**Other flows:**
- Client connects ├втАатАЩ server sends `init` with all current shapes, online users, and current `seq`.
- `cursor` messages ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts to all OTHER clients. Not persisted.
- On connect ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `join` to other clients on the canvas.
- On disconnect ├втАатАЩ server broadcasts `leave`, removes from presence map.

### Cursor Presence Protocol

- Server holds in-memory `dict[str, dict[str, {username, color, ws}]]` keyed by `canvasId` ├втАатАЩ `userId`.
- On connect: assign color from `CURSOR_PALETTE` (round-robin by connection index), broadcast `join`.
- On `cursor` message: broadcast to other clients on same canvas.
- On disconnect: remove from map, broadcast `leave`.
- Cursors are **never persisted**. Presence is purely in-memory.

### Undo/Redo Semantics

**Ownership:** Client-side only. Server has no undo/redo concept ├втВмтАЭ it just processes operations.

**Stacks:** `undoStack: UndoEntry[]`, `redoStack: UndoEntry[]`.

| Local action | forward op | reverse op |
|---|---|---|
| Add shape S | `{kind:"add", shape:S}` | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` |
| Delete shape S | `{kind:"delete", shapeId:S.id}` | `{kind:"add", shape:<snapshot of S before delete>}` |
| Update shape S fields F | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<new values of F>}` | `{kind:"update", shapeId:S.id, props:<old values of F>}` |

**Stack operations:**
- **Do:** push `{forward, reverse}` onto undoStack, clear redoStack, apply forward optimistically, send forward to server.
- **Undo:** pop from undoStack, push to redoStack, apply reverse optimistically, send reverse to server.
- **Redo:** pop from redoStack, push to undoStack, apply forward optimistically, send forward to server.

**Concurrent-edit behavior (field-level LWW):**
- Update reverse ops contain ONLY the fields from the original op. Example: user A changed `fill` ├втАатАЩ reverse is `{props: {fill: oldFill}}`. This never touches `x`, `y`, `width`, `height`, or any other field.
- **Different fields:** If user B moved the shape (changed `x`, `y`) while user A changed `fill`, then A undoes: only `fill` reverts. B's move is preserved. No clobbering.
- **Same field:** If user B also changed `fill`, then A undoes: A's reverse overwrites B's `fill` value. This is accepted LWW behavior for the deployment context (2-5 people on a call, verbal coordination available).
- **Undo add (sends delete):** Deletes the shape even if others have modified it since. Correct ├втВмтАЭ A intended to remove the shape they created.
- **Undo delete (sends add):** Re-adds the shape with A's pre-delete snapshot. Changes made by others between the delete and undo are lost. Accepted for deployment context.
- **Op on non-existent shape:** Server silently ignores. Client's optimistic state self-corrects on reconnect.

### Persistence Boundary

**Persisted (PostgreSQL):**
- Users, canvases, canvas memberships, shapes (current state).

**Not persisted (ephemeral):**
- Cursor positions (server in-memory)
- Undo/redo stacks (client in-memory)
- Canvas `seq` counter (server in-memory, resets on restart ├втВмтАЭ clients get fresh `init` on reconnect)
- Operation history (not stored)
- Online presence (server in-memory)

### Database Schema

Exact DDL for `server/schema.sql`:

```sql
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgcrypto;

CREATE TABLE users (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    username    TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    email       TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    password_hash TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE canvases (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name        TEXT NOT NULL,
    owner_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE canvas_members (
    canvas_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    user_id     UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    PRIMARY KEY (canvas_id, user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE shapes (
    id           UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    canvas_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES canvases(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    type         TEXT NOT NULL,
    x            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    y            DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    width        DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    height       DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    fill         TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
    stroke       TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '#000000',
    stroke_width DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 2,
    text         TEXT,
    font_size    DOUBLE PRECISION
);

CREATE INDEX idx_shapes_canvas ON shapes(canvas_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_canvas_members_user ON canvas_members(user_id);
```

DB uses `snake_case` columns. Wire format uses `camelCase`. Server maps: `stroke_width` ├втАатАЭ `strokeWidth`, `font_size` ├втАатАЭ `fontSize`, `owner_id` ├втАатАЭ `ownerId`, `created_at` ├втАатАЭ `createdAt`.

### Auth / Canvas / Invite API Surface

All HTTP routes under `/api`. All return JSON. Auth-protected routes require `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.

**Auth:**

| Method | Path | Body | Response | Errors |
|--------|------|------|----------|--------|
| POST | `/api/auth/signup` | `SignupRequest` | `AuthResponse` | 409 `username_taken`, `email_taken` |
| POST | `/api/auth/login` | `LoginRequest` | `AuthResponse` | 401 `invalid_credentials` |
| GET | `/api/auth/me` | ├втВмтАЭ | `AuthUser` | 401 `unauthorized` |

**Canvas:**

| Method | Path | Body | Response | Errors |
|--------|------|------|----------|--------|
| GET | `/api/canvases` | ├втВмтАЭ | `CanvasSummary[]` | 401 |
| POST | `/api/canvases` | `CreateCanvasRequest` | `CanvasSummary` | 401 |
| GET | `/api/canvases/:id` | ├втВмтАЭ | `CanvasDetail` | 401, 403 `not_a_member`, 404 `not_found` |
| POST | `/api/canvases/:id/invite` | `InviteRequest` | `{ ok: true }` | 401, 403 `not_a_member`, 404 `user_not_found` / `canvas_not_found` |

Canvas creator is automatically added to `canvas_members`. `GET /api/canvases` returns all canvases where user is a member.

---

## Ownership Map

| Module | Owner | Write Scope | Blocked By | Done When |
|--------|-------|-------------|------------|-----------|
| **infra-runtime** | session-1 | `server/schema.sql`, `server/app/db.py`, `server/app/main.py`, `server/app/deps.py`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `server/Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml` | contract | Schema applies, health check passes, `get_current_user` dep works, CORS configured |
| **backend-auth-canvas** | session-2 | `server/app/auth.py`, `server/app/canvas.py` | infra-runtime | All auth + canvas HTTP endpoints return correct shapes per contract |
| **backend-realtime-persistence** | session-3 | `server/app/ws.py` | infra-runtime | WS connects with auth, sends `init` with seq, echoes ops to ALL with seq+opId, per-canvas lock serializes ops, cursors + presence work |
| **frontend-whiteboard** | session-4 | `client/src/` (all files except `types.ts`) | contract | Auth flow, canvas list/create/invite, shape tools + interactions, undo/redo with opId matching, live cursors, state reloads on refresh |
| **validator** | session-5 | none (read-only) | all builders | PASS/FAIL report for all checks including type parity |

**Execution phases:**
```
Phase 1 (parallel):  infra-runtime  +  frontend-whiteboard
Phase 2 (parallel):  backend-auth-canvas  +  backend-realtime-persistence   ├втАа┬Р after infra only
Phase 3:             validator                                               ├втАа┬Р after all builders
Phase 4:             integration (wire routers into main.py, fix FAILs)
```

**Integration touch points** (Phase 4 only ├втВмтАЭ the only step allowed to cross ownership boundaries):
- Add router imports to `server/app/main.py`: mount `auth_router` at `/api/auth`, `canvas_router` at `/api/canvases`, add WS endpoint from `ws.py`.
- Verify E2E: signup ├втАатАЩ login ├втАатАЩ create canvas ├втАатАЩ invite ├втАатАЩ both connect WS ├втАатАЩ draw ├втАатАЩ see on other client ├втАатАЩ undo ├втАатАЩ refresh ├втАатАЩ shapes persist.

## Naming Rules

**Server (Python):**
- Files: `snake_case.py` in `server/app/`
- Functions: `snake_case` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `create_user`, `get_canvas`
- Route handlers: `snake_case` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `signup`, `login`, `list_canvases`
- Router exports: `auth_router = APIRouter()` in `auth.py`, `canvas_router = APIRouter()` in `canvas.py`
- WebSocket export: `async def websocket_endpoint(ws: WebSocket, ...)` in `ws.py`
- Dependencies: `get_current_user` in `deps.py`, returns `AuthUser` dict
- Imports: absolute from `app` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `from app.types import Shape`, `from app.deps import get_current_user`

**Client (TypeScript):**
- Files: `PascalCase.tsx` for components, `camelCase.ts` for non-components
- Functions/hooks: `camelCase` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `useAuth`, `useCanvas`, `useWebSocket`
- Components: `PascalCase` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `Canvas`, `Toolbar`, `LoginForm`
- Types: imported from `./types.ts`, never redefined locally
- Imports: relative paths with `.tsx`/`.ts` extensions (per tsconfig `allowImportingTsExtensions`)

**Wire format:**
- All JSON keys: `camelCase`
- Error responses: `{ "error": "snake_code" }` ├втВмтАЭ e.g. `"invalid_credentials"`

## Config and Environment

| Variable | Used By | Default | Required |
|----------|---------|---------|----------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | server | `postgres://whiteboard:whiteboard@localhost:5432/whiteboard` | no |
| `JWT_SECRET` | server | (none ├втВмтАЭ must be set, or hardcoded dev fallback like `"dev-secret"`) | yes |
| `VITE_WS_URL` | client | `ws://localhost:3001` | no |
| `VITE_API_URL` | client | `http://localhost:3001` | no |

infra-runtime adds `JWT_SECRET: dev-secret-do-not-use-in-prod` to `docker-compose.yml` server environment.
infra-runtime adds `pyjwt` and `bcrypt` to `pyproject.toml` dependencies.

## Dispatch Prompts

### Session 1: infra-runtime

```
Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md and server/app/types.py.
You own: server/schema.sql, server/app/db.py, server/app/main.py, server/app/deps.py,
         server/pyproject.toml, server/Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml.

1. Write server/schema.sql ├втВмтАЭ exact DDL from ├В┬зDatabase Schema in the contract.
2. Extend server/app/db.py ├втВмтАЭ keep existing pool pattern, no other changes needed.
3. Write server/app/main.py ├втВмтАЭ FastAPI app with lifespan (init/close pool),
   CORSMiddleware allowing http://localhost:5173, health check endpoint (GET /health).
   Do NOT import routers yet ├втВмтАЭ integration step wires them.
4. Write server/app/deps.py ├втВмтАЭ export `get_current_user` FastAPI dependency:
   reads Authorization header, decodes JWT (HS256, JWT_SECRET env var),
   returns AuthUser dict or raises HTTPException(401).
5. Update server/pyproject.toml ├втВмтАЭ add pyjwt>=2.8.0 and bcrypt>=4.0.0 to dependencies.
6. Fix server/Dockerfile ├втВмтАЭ COPY source before pip install, or restructure so build works.
7. Update docker-compose.yml ├втВмтАЭ add JWT_SECRET env var to server service.

Import types from app.types. Never redefine them.
```

### Session 2: backend-auth-canvas

```
Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md, server/app/types.py, server/app/deps.py (for get_current_user).
You own: server/app/auth.py, server/app/canvas.py.

Implement:
1. server/app/auth.py ├втВмтАЭ export auth_router = APIRouter().
   - POST /signup: validate SignupRequest, hash password with bcrypt, INSERT user,
     auto-add to no canvas, return AuthResponse. 409 on duplicate username/email.
   - POST /login: validate LoginRequest, verify bcrypt hash, return AuthResponse. 401 on failure.
   - GET /me: use get_current_user dependency, return AuthUser.
   - JWT payload: {"sub": user_id, "username": username, "exp": now+24h}. Sign with JWT_SECRET.

2. server/app/canvas.py ├втВмтАЭ export canvas_router = APIRouter().
   - GET /: list canvases where user is a member. Return CanvasSummary[].
   - POST /: create canvas, INSERT into canvases + canvas_members (creator auto-member). Return CanvasSummary.
   - GET /{canvas_id}: return CanvasDetail (shapes + members). 403 if not member, 404 if not found.
   - POST /{canvas_id}/invite: look up user by username OR email, add to canvas_members.
     404 if user/canvas not found, 403 if requester not a member.

## Backend-Realtime-Persistence Checks

### R1: WS Auth
Connects at `/ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>`. Validates JWT and canvas membership. Rejects with close 4001 (auth) or 4003 (not member).

### R2: Init Message
On connect sends `{type:"init", shapes:[...], users:[...], seq:<int>}`. Shapes loaded from DB. Users from presence map. `seq` is current canvas counter.

### R3: Op Echo to ALL
On receiving a valid op: persists to DB, increments seq, broadcasts `{type:"op", op, userId, seq, opId}` to **ALL** clients including sender. NOT just others.

### R4: Op Serialization
Ops for a canvas are processed under a per-canvas `asyncio.Lock` (or equivalent). Seq is strictly monotonic per canvas.

### R5: No-Op Handling
If op target doesn't exist (update/delete on missing shape, add on existing id): no broadcast, no seq increment. Silent ignore.

### R6: Persistence
`add` ├втАатАЩ INSERT, `update` ├втАатАЩ UPDATE only specified columns, `delete` ├втАатАЩ DELETE. Parameterized queries, no raw string interpolation.

### R7: Cursor Broadcast
`cursor` messages broadcast to all OTHER clients on the canvas. Not persisted.

### R8: Presence Join/Leave
On connect: assigns color from `CURSOR_PALETTE`, adds to presence, broadcasts `join` to others. On disconnect: removes from presence, broadcasts `leave`.

### R9: Export
Exports `websocket_endpoint` function importable by main.py.

All changes within `server/app/ws.py`. No other files touched.

# AGENTS.md

Use this file for scoped handoffs and post-build review in this repository. Read `CLAUDE.md` first.

## Non-Negotiables

- Stay on the provided stack. Do not swap React, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, or Vite.
- Work on `main`. Commit early and often. Push directly to `main`. No PR workflow.
- Optimize for the actual deployment context: 2-5 users, same canvas, same call, simultaneous edits, 30-90 minute sessions, persistence across sessions.
- Correctness for required features matters more than polishing out-of-scope features.
- Keep collaborative text editing simple. Treat text edits as whole-object updates.
- Prefer simple, explicit conflict behavior over clever distributed systems work. Last-write-wins is acceptable here.

## Shared Review Priorities

Every builder and reviewer should care about these first:

1. Client and server agree on canvas object and websocket payload shapes.
2. Undo and redo semantics are internally consistent and do not corrupt persisted state.
3. Simultaneous edits to the same object have defined behavior.
4. Presence and cursor updates are ephemeral and do not pollute persisted board state.
5. Canvas state reloads correctly across sessions.
6. Authentication and canvas access are enforced on both HTTP and websocket paths.

## Canonical Ownership

If work is split, keep ownership boundaries clean.

### Agent: `infra-runtime`

- Scope:
  - `docker-compose.yml`
  - `client/Dockerfile`
  - `server/Dockerfile`
  - `client/vite.config.ts`
  - `SETUP.md`
- Responsibilities:
  - deterministic local startup
  - Docker networking fixes
  - health and readiness behavior
  - environment-variable wiring
- Must not own:
  - whiteboard domain logic
  - websocket protocol design
  - client canvas behavior

### Agent: `backend-domain`

- Scope:
  - `server/app/**`
  - `server/schema.sql`
- Responsibilities:
  - auth endpoints
  - canvas CRUD and invite flow
  - websocket protocol
  - persistence model
  - undo/redo persistence semantics
  - cursor broadcast and board event handling
- Must not own:
  - React component structure
  - Vite or Docker behavior unless blocked by backend bring-up

### Agent: `frontend-whiteboard`

- Scope:
  - `client/src/**`
  - client-only package updates if required
- Responsibilities:
  - auth screens and session state
  - canvas UI
  - shape creation and manipulation
  - remote cursor rendering
  - websocket client integration
  - undo and redo UX
- Must not own:
  - backend protocol changes without updating the shared contract first
  - Docker or DB changes unless explicitly reassigned

### Agent: `reviewer`

- Scope:
  - read-only across the whole repo
- Responsibilities:
  - code review after feature work
  - identify regressions, risks, missing validation, and protocol mismatches
  - verify required features against the take-home rubric
- Must not do:
  - opportunistic refactors
  - style-only feedback unless style causes defects
  - implementation work unless explicitly asked

## Handoff Requirements

Any handoff should include:

- exact write scope
- files changed
- public exports added or changed
- API routes added or changed
- websocket message types added or changed
- schema changes
- follow-up blockers
- what still needs validation

Use this format:

```md
## Handoff
- Scope:
- Files changed:
- Routes changed:
- Websocket messages changed:
- Schema changes:
- New env vars:
- Risks or blockers:
- Next validator checks:
```

## Builder Prompt Template

```text
Read CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md first.
Own only [write-scope].
Implement [task].
Preserve the provided stack and current repo conventions.
Do not change shapes, websocket payloads, or persistence semantics outside your scope without calling it out explicitly.
At the end, provide a handoff using the AGENTS.md format.
```

## Reviewer Prompt Template

```text
Read CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md first.
Review the finished implementation in a code-review mindset.
Prioritize bugs, robustness gaps, behavioral regressions, protocol mismatches, missing validation, and missing tests.
Focus on the actual take-home requirements and the synchronous-collaboration deployment context.
Do not fix code. Report findings first, ordered by severity, with file references.
```

## Whiteboard-Specific Review Checklist

The reviewer should explicitly check:

- accounts: signup, login, logout actually work
- canvas access: create, open, invite work end to end
- persistence: reloading a canvas restores the latest state
- shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, and text all round-trip correctly
- manipulation: select, move, resize, recolor, delete work locally and remotely
- collaboration: two users see each other's edits promptly
- cursor presence: cursors render for other users and disappear on disconnect
- websocket auth: unauthorized users cannot attach to arbitrary canvases
- undo and redo: behavior is predictable under concurrent edits
- conflict handling: same-object edits do not crash or corrupt board state
- reconnect behavior: stale or disconnected clients do not leave broken presence state behind

## Recommended Review Order

1. `server/schema.sql`
2. `server/app/**` auth, canvas, invite, websocket, persistence
3. `client/src/**` state model and websocket integration
4. cross-check shared payload shapes between client and server
5. Docker and local run flow

## Stop Conditions

Pause and escalate instead of pushing ahead when:

- client and server payload shapes diverge
- undo and redo behavior is undefined under concurrent edits
- websocket messages are added ad hoc without a stable envelope
- auth is implemented for HTTP but not websocket access
- canvas persistence and live collaboration use different shape models

## Notes For This Repo

- The current scaffold is minimal. Expect most required behavior to be built from scratch.
- `server/schema.sql` is the canonical place for DDL.
- `asyncpg` uses `$1`, `$2` placeholders. Do not use string-formatted SQL.
- The frontend should talk through `/api/*` and `/ws`, not hardcoded server ports.
- If a contract file is introduced later, treat it as architect-owned and keep reviewers focused on contract compliance.
