content.text:
# Review findings:

## Finding 1 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//server//app//ws.py:202-226) [added]
[P2] Bad websocket canvas IDs still fail before clean rejection

The integrated websocket route is mounted, but it still sends the raw `canvasId` string into the UUID-backed membership query without validating it first. A malformed canvas URL can trigger an asyncpg conversion failure before the socket is accepted, yielding an internal failure instead of a clean membership or auth rejection.

## Finding 2 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:615-624) [added]
[P0] Integration still violates server-authoritative op ordering

The client applies local ops optimistically and then simply drops the echoed op if the `opId` matches. If a conflicting remote op arrives before that echo, this client keeps its local-first ordering instead of the server's `seq` ordering and can diverge from the authoritative board state. Session 5 wired the stack together, but it did not resolve this core cross-client consistency bug.

## Finding 3 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:121-135) [added]
[P1] Integrated UI can show edits that never reach the server

`sendOp` mutates local state and records the op as pending before confirming the websocket is open. When the socket is still connecting or has dropped, the UI shows the change locally but nothing is sent or retried, so the change disappears on refresh and never reaches collaborators. This is now an integration bug because the feature appears usable in the wired app but is not durable.

## Finding 4 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:573-578) [added]
[P1] HTTP bootstrap can overwrite fresher websocket state

The canvas detail fetch and websocket initialization both assign to `shapesRef.current` without coordination. If the GET resolves after websocket `init` or after live ops, the older HTTP snapshot can clobber newer collaborative state on this client. Session 5 connected the flows, but it did not gate or serialize them.

## My request for Codex:
run review for whole code base all sessions

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)

- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.

- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

# Review findings:

## Finding 1 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//server//app//ws.py:202-226) [added]
[P2] Malformed websocket canvas IDs are not rejected cleanly

The websocket handler still passes the raw `canvasId` string into the UUID-backed membership query without validating it first. A bad canvas id can fail inside asyncpg before the socket is accepted, producing an internal failure instead of a clean rejection.

## Finding 2 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:615-624) [added]
[P0] Client can diverge from server-authoritative op order

The client applies its own op immediately and then drops the echoed op if the `opId` matches. If a conflicting remote op arrives first, this client preserves local-first ordering instead of the server's `seq` ordering, so peers can end up with different final state for the same shape. This is the main correctness issue left in the integrated system.

## Finding 3 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:121-135) [added]
[P1] Local edits are lost when the websocket is not open

`sendOp` mutates local state and records the op as pending before confirming that the websocket is open. If the socket is still connecting or has dropped, the user sees the edit locally but it is never sent or retried, so it disappears on refresh and never reaches collaborators.

## Finding 4 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:573-578) [added]
[P1] HTTP bootstrap can overwrite newer realtime state

The initial canvas detail fetch and websocket initialization both assign to `shapesRef.current` without coordination. If the HTTP request resolves after websocket `init` or after live ops, the older snapshot can clobber fresher collaborative state on this client.

  if (isOwnEcho && msg.op.kind === "add") {

  } else {
    shapesRef.current = applyOp(shapesRef.current, msg.op);
  }

  It only skips own-echo adds (safe тАФ no one can conflict on a UUID you just generated). For own-echo updates  
  and deletes, it re-applies the server's canonical version. Since TCP preserves in-order delivery within a    
  single WS connection, ops always arrive in seq order. This guarantees convergence.

    (op: Operation) => {

  getCanvasDetail(canvasId)
    .then((detail) => {
      if (!wsInitReceivedRef.current) {  // тЖР guard
        shapesRef.current = detail.shapes;

  And line 613 sets the flag before assigning WS shapes:
  case "init":
    wsInitReceivedRef.current = true;
    shapesRef.current = msg.shapes;

  HTTP response is ignored if WS init already arrived. No stale overwrite.

  ---
  P2 тАФ malformed canvasId: FALSE POSITIVE

  The integration fix at lines 231-236 already handles this:

  try:
      cid = _uuid.UUID(canvas_id)
      uid = _uuid.UUID(user_id)
  except (ValueError, AttributeError):
      await ws.close(code=4001, reason="invalid canvasId or userId")
      return

  The membership query at lines 238-243 uses cid and uid (UUID objects), not the raw strings. A malformed      
  canvasId gets a clean 4001 rejection before any DB query runs.

  ---
  All 4 findings are false positives. The session-4 frontend builder and session-5 integration work already    
  addressed each concern. No code changes needed.

"""
WebSocket handler for real-time whiteboard collaboration.

Session-3 scope: server/app/ws.py only.
Implements: auth, init, op persistence+broadcast with seq/opId,
cursor relay, presence join/leave, per-canvas locking.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import json
import os
import uuid as _uuid
from collections import defaultdict

from fastapi import WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

from app.db import get_pool
from app.types import (
    CURSOR_PALETTE,
    SHAPE_DB_TO_WIRE,
    SHAPE_WIRE_TO_DB,
    AuthUser,
    PresenceUser,
)

try:
    import jwt
except ImportError:  # pragma: no cover ├втВмтАЬ pyjwt is a runtime dep
    raise

# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Module-level state (per-process, single worker) ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм

# canvas_id -> user_id -> {username, color, ws, send_lock}
presence: dict[str, dict[str, dict]] = defaultdict(dict)

# canvas_id -> monotonically increasing int
seq_counters: dict[str, int] = defaultdict(int)

# canvas_id -> asyncio.Lock (serialises op processing per canvas)
canvas_locks: dict[str, asyncio.Lock] = defaultdict(asyncio.Lock)


# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Helpers ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


def _decode_token(token: str) -> AuthUser | None:
    """Decode a JWT token manually (no FastAPI DI for WebSocket)."""
    secret = os.environ.get("JWT_SECRET", "dev-secret-do-not-use-in-prod")
    if not secret:
        return None
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, secret, algorithms=["HS256"])
    except (jwt.ExpiredSignatureError, jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        return None
    sub = payload.get("sub")
    username = payload.get("username")
    email = payload.get("email")
    if not sub or not username:
        return None
    return AuthUser(id=sub, username=username, email=email or "")


def _row_to_shape(row: dict) -> dict:
    """Convert an asyncpg Record (snake_case) to a wire-format Shape (camelCase)."""
    shape: dict = {}
    for key, value in row.items():
        if key in ("canvas_id",):
            continue  # internal, not on the wire
        # Omit None for optional fields (text, font_size) so the wire format
        # matches the NotRequired TypedDict contract.
        if value is None:
            continue
        wire_key = SHAPE_DB_TO_WIRE.get(key, key)
        # UUID -> str
        if hasattr(value, "hex"):
            shape[wire_key] = str(value)
        else:
            shape[wire_key] = value
    return shape


_ALLOWED_SHAPE_COLS = frozenset({
    "id", "type", "x", "y", "width", "height",
    "fill", "stroke", "stroke_width", "text", "font_size",
})


def _shape_to_columns(shape: dict) -> tuple[list[str], list]:
    """Extract DB columns and values from a wire-format Shape dict for INSERT.

    Only whitelisted column names are accepted to prevent SQL injection
    through crafted key names in the shape payload.
    """
    cols: list[str] = []
    vals: list = []
    for wire_key, value in shape.items():
        db_key = SHAPE_WIRE_TO_DB.get(wire_key, wire_key)
        if db_key not in _ALLOWED_SHAPE_COLS:
            continue
        # asyncpg requires uuid.UUID for UUID columns
        if db_key == "id" and isinstance(value, str):
            value = _uuid.UUID(value)
        cols.append(db_key)
        vals.append(value)
    return cols, vals


async def _safe_send(ws: WebSocket, data: str, send_lock: asyncio.Lock) -> None:
    """Send text through a WebSocket, serialised per-connection to avoid frame interleaving."""
    try:
        async with send_lock:
            await ws.send_text(data)
    except Exception:
        pass  # connection already closed; disconnect handler will clean up


async def _broadcast(canvas_id: str, message: dict, *, exclude_user: str | None = None) -> None:
    """Broadcast a JSON message to all clients on a canvas.

    Uses asyncio.gather with return_exceptions=True so one slow/dead
    client never blocks the others (CLAUDE.md gotcha #2).
    """
    payload = json.dumps(message)
    targets = []
    for uid, info in presence.get(canvas_id, {}).items():
        if uid == exclude_user:
            continue
        targets.append(_safe_send(info["ws"], payload, info["send_lock"]))
    if targets:
        await asyncio.gather(*targets, return_exceptions=True)


async def _broadcast_all(canvas_id: str, message: dict) -> None:
    """Broadcast to ALL clients on a canvas, including the sender."""
    payload = json.dumps(message)
    targets = []
    for _uid, info in presence.get(canvas_id, {}).items():
        targets.append(_safe_send(info["ws"], payload, info["send_lock"]))
    if targets:
        await asyncio.gather(*targets, return_exceptions=True)


# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Op persistence ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


async def _persist_add(conn, canvas_id: str, shape: dict) -> bool:
    """INSERT a new shape. Returns True if inserted, False if id already existed."""
    cols, vals = _shape_to_columns(shape)
    # Ensure canvas_id is included (as UUID for asyncpg)
    if "canvas_id" not in cols:
        cols.append("canvas_id")
        vals.append(_uuid.UUID(canvas_id) if isinstance(canvas_id, str) else canvas_id)
    placeholders = ", ".join(f"${i+1}" for i in range(len(cols)))
    col_names = ", ".join(cols)
    result = await conn.execute(
        f"INSERT INTO shapes ({col_names}) VALUES ({placeholders}) ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING",
        *vals,
    )
    # asyncpg returns 'INSERT 0 1' or 'INSERT 0 0'
    return result.endswith("1")


_ALLOWED_UPDATE_COLS = _ALLOWED_SHAPE_COLS - {"id", "type"}


async def _persist_update(conn, canvas_id: str, shape_id: str, props: dict) -> bool:
    """UPDATE only the columns in props, scoped to this canvas.

    Returns True if a row was modified.
    """
    if not props:
        return False
    set_parts: list[str] = []
    vals: list = []
    idx = 1
    for wire_key, value in props.items():
        db_key = SHAPE_WIRE_TO_DB.get(wire_key, wire_key)
        if db_key not in _ALLOWED_UPDATE_COLS:
            continue
        set_parts.append(f"{db_key} = ${idx}")
        vals.append(value)
        idx += 1
    if not set_parts:
        return False
    vals.append(_uuid.UUID(shape_id) if isinstance(shape_id, str) else shape_id)
    vals.append(_uuid.UUID(canvas_id) if isinstance(canvas_id, str) else canvas_id)
    sql = f"UPDATE shapes SET {', '.join(set_parts)} WHERE id = ${idx} AND canvas_id = ${idx + 1}"
    result = await conn.execute(sql, *vals)
    # asyncpg returns 'UPDATE N'
    return not result.endswith("0")


async def _persist_delete(conn, canvas_id: str, shape_id: str) -> bool:
    """DELETE a shape, scoped to this canvas. Returns True if a row was removed."""
    result = await conn.execute(
        "DELETE FROM shapes WHERE id = $1 AND canvas_id = $2",
        _uuid.UUID(shape_id) if isinstance(shape_id, str) else shape_id,
        _uuid.UUID(canvas_id) if isinstance(canvas_id, str) else canvas_id,
    )
    return not result.endswith("0")


# ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Main endpoint ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм


async def websocket_endpoint(ws: WebSocket) -> None:
    """WebSocket endpoint for a canvas session.

    Connection: WS /ws?canvasId=<uuid>&token=<jwt>
    """
    # ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Auth before accept ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм
    canvas_id = ws.query_params.get("canvasId", "")
    token = ws.query_params.get("token", "")

    if not canvas_id or not token:
        await ws.close(code=4001, reason="missing canvasId or token")
        return

    user = _decode_token(token)
    if user is None:
        await ws.close(code=4001, reason="auth failure")
        return

    user_id: str = user["id"]
    username: str = user["username"]

    # Verify canvas membership
    pool = await get_pool()
    try:
        cid = _uuid.UUID(canvas_id)
        uid = _uuid.UUID(user_id)
    except (ValueError, AttributeError):
        await ws.close(code=4001, reason="invalid canvasId or userId")
        return

    async with pool.acquire(timeout=5) as conn:
        row = await conn.fetchrow(
            "SELECT 1 FROM canvas_members WHERE canvas_id = $1 AND user_id = $2",
            cid,
            uid,
        )
    if row is None:
        await ws.close(code=4003, reason="not a canvas member")
        return

    # ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Accept ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм
    await ws.accept()

    # Per-connection send lock to prevent frame interleaving (CLAUDE.md gotcha #5)
    send_lock = asyncio.Lock()

    # ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Presence ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм
    canvas_presence = presence[canvas_id]
    color_index = len(canvas_presence) % len(CURSOR_PALETTE)
    color = CURSOR_PALETTE[color_index]

    me: PresenceUser = PresenceUser(
        userId=user_id,
        username=username,
        color=color,
    )
    canvas_presence[user_id] = {
        "username": username,
        "color": color,
        "ws": ws,
        "send_lock": send_lock,
    }

    try:
        # ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Send init ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм
        async with pool.acquire(timeout=5) as conn:
            rows = await conn.fetch(
                "SELECT * FROM shapes WHERE canvas_id = $1",
                cid,
            )
        shapes = [_row_to_shape(dict(r)) for r in rows]
        users = [
            PresenceUser(userId=uid, username=info["username"], color=info["color"])
            for uid, info in canvas_presence.items()
        ]
        current_seq = seq_counters[canvas_id]

        init_msg = json.dumps({
            "type": "init",
            "shapes": shapes,
            "users": users,
            "seq": current_seq,
        })
        async with send_lock:
            await ws.send_text(init_msg)

        # Broadcast join to others
        await _broadcast(canvas_id, {"type": "join", "user": dict(me)}, exclude_user=user_id)

        # ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Message loop ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм
        while True:
            raw = await ws.receive_text()
            try:
                msg = json.loads(raw)
            except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
                continue  # ignore malformed messages

            msg_type = msg.get("type")

            if msg_type == "op":
                op = msg.get("op")
                op_id = msg.get("opId", "")
                if not op or not isinstance(op, dict):
                    continue

                kind = op.get("kind")
                lock = canvas_locks[canvas_id]

                # Acquire per-canvas lock, persist, assign seq, then
                # release before broadcast (contract steps 2-7).
                modified = False
                seq = 0
                async with lock:
                    async with pool.acquire(timeout=5) as conn:
                        if kind == "add":
                            shape = op.get("shape")
                            if shape and isinstance(shape, dict) and "id" in shape:
                                modified = await _persist_add(conn, canvas_id, shape)
                        elif kind == "update":
                            shape_id = op.get("shapeId")
                            props = op.get("props")
                            if shape_id and props and isinstance(props, dict):
                                modified = await _persist_update(conn, canvas_id, shape_id, props)
                        elif kind == "delete":
                            shape_id = op.get("shapeId")
                            if shape_id:
                                modified = await _persist_delete(conn, canvas_id, shape_id)
                    if modified:
                        seq_counters[canvas_id] += 1
                        seq = seq_counters[canvas_id]
                # Lock released ├втВмтАЭ now broadcast outside the lock
                if modified:
                    await _broadcast_all(canvas_id, {
                        "type": "op",
                        "op": op,
                        "userId": user_id,
                        "seq": seq,
                        "opId": op_id,
                    })
                # If not modified (no-op): no broadcast, no seq increment

            elif msg_type == "cursor":
                x = msg.get("x")
                y = msg.get("y")
                if x is not None and y is not None:
                    await _broadcast(canvas_id, {
                        "type": "cursor",
                        "userId": user_id,
                        "username": username,
                        "x": x,
                        "y": y,
                    }, exclude_user=user_id)

    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        pass
    except Exception:
        # Catch-all: don't let unexpected errors leave presence dirty
        pass
    finally:
        # ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Cleanup ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм
        canvas_presence.pop(user_id, None)

        # Broadcast leave to remaining clients
        await _broadcast(canvas_id, {"type": "leave", "userId": user_id})

        # Clean up empty canvas entries
        if not canvas_presence:
            presence.pop(canvas_id, None)
            seq_counters.pop(canvas_id, None)
            canvas_locks.pop(canvas_id, None)

YouтАЩre looking at a newer snapshot than the one I reviewed earlier.

I re-checked the current files, and the 4 quoted findings are stale on the code that is on disk now:

- WebSocket `canvasId` validation is now present before the DB query in [ws.py](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\server\app\ws.py#L231).
- `sendOp` now bails before mutating local state if the socket is not open in [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L129).
- HTTP bootstrap is now guarded by `wsInitReceivedRef` in [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L71) and [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L576).
- Own-echo handling now only skips `add`; `update` and `delete` echoes are reapplied in [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L621).

So for those 4 specific findings: the other agent is right, and my earlier review is outdated.

There is still one nearby issue, though: [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L143) pushes undo history before [sendOp](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L129) can reject a disconnected socket. That means you no longer get phantom board edits, but you can still get bogus undo stack entries while disconnected.

So the short answer is: the discrepancy happened because the code changed after the review.

- `sendOp` now bails before mutating local state if the socket is not open in [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L129).
- HTTP bootstrap is now guarded by `wsInitReceivedRef` in [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L71) and [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L576).

So for those 4 specific findings: the other agent is right, and my earlier review is outdated.

There is still one nearby issue, though: [CanvasPage.tsx](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L143) pushes undo history before [sendOp](C:\Users\sahil\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Mechanize_takehome\client\src\components\CanvasPage.tsx#L129) can reject a disconnected socket. settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
How do we use batch on Claude code.

4:56 PM

Assembled sufficient knowledge to address batch functionality
Assembled sufficient knowledge to address batch functionality
The /batch command in Claude Code is a slash command for parallelizing large-scale codebase changes. Here's how it works:

Basic usage тАФ just type /batch followed by a natural language description of what you want:

/batch migrate from React to Vue
/batch add type annotations to all untyped functions
/batch replace all uses of lodash with native equivalents
How it works under the hood:

When executed, an internal orchestrator agent launches and processes the work in three phases. 
SmartScope
 It explores your codebase, breaks your task into discrete units тАФ one per file or one per defined scope тАФ and runs them concurrently using multiple Claude instances. 
MindStudio
 Typically it decomposes into 5тАУ30 independent units. Each worker automatically runs /simplify on its own changes before creating PRs. 
Pasquale Pillitteri

When to use it:

Library/framework migrations
Dependency swaps
Bulk test generation
Renaming conventions across many files
Adding error handling project-wide
When NOT to use it:

Tightly coupled changes where file A depends on file B's new output aren't independent units. Use standard Claude Code sessions or agent teams for coordinated multi-agent work instead. 
Claude Fast

Tips for good results:

Be specific about scope тАФ "all TypeScript files in /src/api" beats "the API folder." Define the transformation precisely so Claude can apply it consistently. 
MindStudio

Requirements: Claude Code v2.1.63 or higher. Check with claude --version and update with claude update if needed. 
Pasquale Pillitteri

Note this is different from the Batch API (for sending bulk API requests at 50% cost) and from headless mode (claude -p) which is for CI/CD integration. /batch is specifically for interactive parallel codebase migrations.

What's the latest in claude code.

4:57 PM

Synthesized Claude Code developments for concise overview
Synthesized Claude Code developments for concise overview
Here's a rundown of what's been shipping in Claude Code through March 2026:

Major features:

Push-to 
Pasquale Pillitteri
-talk voice mode (/voice) is rolling out progressively тАФ hold spacebar to speak, release to send. Currently available to ~5% of users.
/loop command turns Claude Code into a recurring monitor тАФ e.g., /loop 5m check the deploy runs every 5 minutes as a lightweight session-level cron job. 
Pasquale Pillitteri
1M context window for Opus 4.6 is now default for Max, Team, and Enterprise plans. 
GitHub
Opus 4.6 output limits increased to 64k default, 128k upper bound (Sonnet 4.6 also gets 128k ceiling). 
Claude
/batch and /simplify bundled skills shipped in v2.1.63 for parallel codebase migrations and automated 3-agent code review.
Flicker-free alt-screen rendering via CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1, plus named subagents in @ mention typeahead. 
Releasebot
Operational improvements:

--bare flag for scripted -p calls that skips hooks, LSP, plugin sync. --channels permission relay can forward approval prompts to your phone. 
Builder.io
Rate limit info in the statusline (5-hour and 7-day windows with usage percentage and reset time). 
Claude Fast
/color command to set prompt-bar color per session, last-modified timestamps on memory files, hook source display in permission prompts. 
GitHub
Cloud auto-fix can now follow PRs, fix CI failures, and address review comments while you're away. 
Builder.io
The bigger picture: Claude Code is increasingly shaped for sustained, long-running agent workflows тАФ persistent sessions, scheduled tasks, remote continuity, and outputs people can actually review. 
Builder.io

Update with claude update or npm update -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code.

Check same for codex

5:00 PM

Assembled Codex update information for review
Assembled Codex update information for review
Here's where Codex stands as of late March 2026:

Models:

** 
OpenAI
GPT-5.2-Codex** is the current flagship тАФ state-of-the-art on SWE-Bench Pro and Terminal-Bench 2.0, with stronger long-context understanding, native compaction, and significantly better cybersecurity capabilities.
GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark just launched in research preview (partnership with Cerebras) тАФ text-only, 128k context, available in the app/CLI/IDE extension for ChatGPT Pro users. 
Openai
GPT-5.4 mini landed March 5 тАФ positioned as the cheaper/faster model for parallel subagents while GPT-5.4 handles planning and final judgment. 
LaoZhang AI Blog
Major March features:

Plugins are now first-class тАФ Codex can sync product-scoped plugins at startup, browse them in /plugins, and connect to tools like Sentry and Datadog. 
Apiyi.com Blog
Triggers тАФ event-driven automation that fires on GitHub events (issue created, PR opened, etc.) without polling. OpenAI positions this as an always-on engineering partner. 
Apiyi.com Blog
 This is a notable gap vs. Claude Code's polling-based /loop.
Codex Security entered research preview for application security review. 
Apiyi.com Blog
Windows release expanded platform coverage beyond macOS. 
Apiyi.com Blog
Multi-agent v2 тАФ sub-agents now use readable path-based addresses like /root/agent_a with structured inter-agent messaging. 
GitHub
Product surface improvements:

Thread search, one-click local thread archiving, synced settings between the app and VS Code extension. 
Releasebot
Custom theming, automations that can run locally or on worktrees, custom reasoning levels, and templates. 
Releasebot
codex exec now supports prompt-plus-stdin workflow for piping input with a separate prompt. 
Openai
The big picture take from analysts: March was the month Codex stopped looking like a loose bundle of features and started looking like a coherent agent stack тАФ the app for parallel agents, tiered models for cost/capability routing, event-driven automation, and a clearer local-vs-cloud runtime story. 
LaoZhang AI Blog

The competitive framing vs. Claude Code: Codex is moving toward unattended, fully autonomous agents (Triggers + Sandbox), while Claude Code is focusing on deep developer collaboration (reasoning + desktop control + voice + mobile remote). 
Apiyi.com Blog
 Different philosophies, converging capabilities.

If I have to implement a major prd in 150 minutes, how do we get there.

5:22 PM

Devised rapid execution strategy for time-constrained implementation
Devised rapid execution strategy for time-constrained implementation
Here's the 150-minute playbook assuming Claude Code (or Codex, but CC's /batch is better for this pattern):

Minutes 0тАУ15: Scaffold & decompose

Drop the PRD into Claude Code as context. Ask it to decompose into independent modules тАФ data models, API routes, core business logic, UI components. Get a CLAUDE.md written with architecture decisions, tech stack, file structure, and test expectations. This is your planning phase тАФ don't skip it, it saves you 30+ minutes downstream.

Minutes 15тАУ30: Skeleton generation

Have Claude Code generate the full project skeleton тАФ directory structure, config files, DB schema, type definitions, shared interfaces. Everything downstream depends on these contracts being right. Review and correct manually here. This is the highest-leverage 15 minutes.

Minutes 30тАУ90: Parallel build with /batch

This is where you get the 5-10x multiplier. Use /batch to fan out independent units: each API endpoint, each UI component, each service module. Since /batch decomposes into 5тАУ30 parallel workers and each runs /simplify before committing, you get reviewed code out of each worker. Feed it specific instructions тАФ "implement the auth middleware per the types in src/types/auth.ts" beats "add auth."

For anything with cross-module dependencies (e.g., a service that needs another service's output), handle those sequentially in the main session after the batch completes.

Minutes 90тАУ120: Integration & wiring

Wire the modules together. This is where you're back in a normal Claude Code session тАФ connecting routes to controllers, hooking up the frontend to the API, resolving import paths, fixing the inevitable interface mismatches from parallel generation. Run the app. Fix what breaks.

Minutes 120тАУ140: Test & harden

Use /batch again to fan out test generation across modules. Then run the full suite, fix failures in the main session. If you have CI, push and let it validate.

Minutes 140тАУ150: Polish & ship

/simplify on your diff. Clean up dead code, fix naming inconsistencies. Commit, push, open PR.

Tactical tips:

Pin your model to Opus 4.6 for the planning/integration phases, Sonnet 4.6 for the batch workers if you're hitting rate limits
Commit after each phase so you have rollback points
Don't iterate on UI polish during the build тАФ get functional first
If a /batch worker produces something wrong, fix it manually rather than re-running тАФ faster at this timescale
Keep a scratch file of decisions and deviations from the PRD so you don't lose track

Why it works:

The structure that actually holds up:

markdown
# BUILD CONTRACT тАФ [Feature Name]
## Status: IN PROGRESS

## Architecture Decisions (DO NOT MODIFY)
- Framework: Next.js 14 + FastAPI
- DB: Postgres via SQLAlchemy
- Auth: JWT with refresh tokens

## Interface Contracts (DO NOT MODIFY)
- POST /api/sessions тЖТ { session_id, token }
- GET /api/sessions/:id тЖТ { status, transcript }
- WebSocket /ws/session/:id тЖТ real-time events

## Tasks
- [x] Schema + types (@claude-code, done)
- [ ] Auth middleware (@codex, in-progress)
- [ ] Session API endpoints (@claude-code, blocked:auth)
- [ ] Frontend session view (@claude-code-batch)
- [ ] WebSocket handler (@codex)
- [ ] Integration wiring (@claude-code, waiting:all)

## Shared Types (source of truth)
```typescript
// Both agents MUST use these exactly
interface Session { id: string; status: ... }
```

## Completion Signals
- When done, mark task [x] and add output path
- If blocked, note what you're blocked on
- Do NOT modify another agent's completed work
How you run it:

Write the checklist with contracts and types locked at the top
Tell Claude Code: "Read BUILD_CONTRACT.md. Pick up tasks marked @claude-code. Update status when done. Do not touch @codex tasks."
Tell Codex: same instruction, flipped assignments
You commit between rounds so both see updated state
You handle the integration task yourself in Claude Code once all pieces land
The gotchas:

Git conflicts on the markdown file itself. Both agents will try to update it. Solution: you're the one who commits and pulls. Agents write to their own status files (claude-status.md, codex-status.md) and you merge into the contract.
Type drift. One agent subtly changes a return shape. Solution: put shared types in an actual .ts or .py file that both import from, not just in the markdown. The markdown references it, the code enforces it.
Ordering ambiguity. "Blocked:auth" means nothing if the auth task output isn't specified. Solution: every task needs an explicit output artifact тАФ file path, endpoint, or export name.
The upgrade path if you want to get serious: Put a CLAUDE.md and a .codex file in the repo root that both point to the contract file as the first thing to read. That way every new session/thread from either tool starts contract-aware without you repeating instructions.

This won't be seamless тАФ you're the orchestrator, not either agent. But for a 150-minute sprint it cuts your integration phase from 30 minutes to maybe 10 because both agents built against the same interface spec instead of hallucinating their own.

- Validators: validate{Resource}{Field} тЖТ validateSessionToken

- PATCH /api/{resource}/:id тЖТ update
- DELETE /api/{resource}/:id тЖТ delete

Yes. Create a skill, not a model.

A new model is the wrong lever here unless youтАЩre doing actual fine-tuning on a large, repetitive classification or generation task. Your problem is orchestration, contracts, task routing, validation, and handoff discipline. That belongs in a skill or playbook.

What you want is:

1. A reusable operating model
2. A repo contract template
3. A validator protocol
4. A dispatch protocol for builder vs reviewer agents

That can live as a Codex skill very cleanly.

**What the skill should do**

Name it something like `contract-first-swarm` or `dual-agent-prd-sprint`.

Its job would be to take a PRD and produce:

- `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- `src/types/shared.ts` or equivalent shared contract file
- `status/` schema
- `validation-instructions.md`
- ownership map by directory
- prompts for builder agents
- prompts for validator agents
- stop rules for integration

So the skill is not тАЬwrite code fasterтАЭ in the abstract. It is тАЬnormalize the first 15 minutes so all later agent work composes.тАЭ

**Why a skill beats a model**

A skill gives you:

- repeatable setup
- explicit instructions
- files and templates
- guardrails
- a workflow both Codex and Claude can follow

A model would not solve:

- naming drift
- return-shape mismatch
- ownership conflicts
- unclear status handoff
- bad validation criteria

Those are protocol problems.

**Best structure**

IтАЩd split it into two layers:

`1. Skill`
This is the reusable brain for Codex.

It should encode:
- how to read a PRD
- how to extract modules
- how to lock conventions
- how to generate shared contracts
- how to split work into independent ownership zones
- how to prepare validator instructions
- how to decide what is human-only

`2. Repo-local contract pack`
This is per project.

It would contain:
- `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- `validation-instructions.md`
- `CLAUDE.md`
- `.codex` or Codex-facing instructions
- `status/README.md`

The skill generates or updates this pack.

**The core principle**

The skill should force this sequence:

1. Read PRD
2. Normalize requirements into contracts
3. Freeze naming, types, API shape, and directory ownership
4. Only then dispatch builders
5. Run validation against contract, not vibes
6. Only one actor handles integration

That is the real speedup.

**What I would not do**

so for contracts i can set it to half codex half claude? and let codex build codex and claude build claude??\

No. Do not split the contract 50/50.

That is the one thing that should have a single owner.

**Good split**
- One tool drafts the contract
- The other tool reviews or red-teams the contract
- You approve the final version
- Then both can build against it in separate scopes

**Bad split**
- Claude writes half of `BUILD_CONTRACT.md`
- Codex writes the other half
- Both invent types, routes, names, and ownership rules independently

That creates drift immediately.

The right pattern is:

1. Claude or Codex creates the full contract pack.
2. The other tool audits it and points out gaps.
3. You lock the final contract.
4. Then split implementation:
   - Claude builds modules A and B
   - Codex builds modules C and D
5. Validator checks all modules against the same frozen contract.
6. One integration owner wires the final result.

So:
- `contract`: single writer, second reviewer
- `implementation`: many writers, disjoint scopes
- `validation`: separate from builders
- `integration`: single owner

If you really want both involved in contract creation, the safe version is:
- Claude writes contract draft 1
- Codex writes review comments or a competing draft
- you merge to one final contract
- after that, nobody edits it casually

**Short rule**
DonтАЩt split the spec.
Split the work built from the spec.

A strong setup would be:
- Claude: contract architect + builder for some modules
- Codex: contract reviewer + builder for different modules
- Codex or Claude fresh session: validator
- one tool only: integration

README
Mechanize Take-Home: Collaborative Whiteboard
Thank you for taking the time to interview with Mechanize! This repo contains your assignment for the final phase of our interview process, an open-ended take-home assignment.

The take-home begins with a solo work block of three hours in which you can complete the task as described below.

There is a 20-minute break after the solo block. During this time, our team will review your submission. If we'd like to move forward based on your progress, we'll talk to you on the follow-up call. If not, we'll cancel the follow-up calendar invite and send you an email cancelling the call.

Getting started
Clone this repo (do not fork it):
git clone <this-repo-url>
cd <this-repo-name>
See SETUP.md for how to run the scaffold.
Do all of your work on the main branch. Commit early and often.
Push your commits directly to main тАФ do not open a pull request.
git push origin main
Your final commit must be pushed no later than three hours after your start time. Anything pushed after the deadline will not be considered.
What you're building
A real-time collaborative whiteboard. Multiple users, same canvas, editing together.

Deployment context
This whiteboard is for synchronous design reviews, consisting of a small group (typically 2-5 people) on a call together, using the canvas as a shared scratchpad while they talk through a design.

Each user has an account and can create canvases or be invited to others'.
It's common for several people to be editing at once -- often the same objects -- as they try alternatives or work through a disagreement in real time.
A typical session runs 30-90 minutes.
Teams sometimes return to a canvas in a later session to recall what was decided.
Required features
User accounts (signup, login, logout)
Create a canvas, open an existing canvas
Invite other users to a canvas (by username or email -- a simple mechanism is fine)
Shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, text
Select, move, resize, delete, change color
Undo / redo
See other users' cursors
Canvas state persists across sessions
Out of scope
Don't spend time on these feature, as we won't evaluate them:

Collaborative text editing within text elements (treating a text update as a single operation is fine -- you don't need to handle two users typing in the same text box simultaneously)
Granular permissions (viewer/editor/owner roles, share-with-link, etc.)
Rich text editing, images, or file uploads
Canvas zoom, pan, or infinite canvas
Export (PNG, SVG, PDF)
Version history or named snapshots
Mobile or touch support
Provided scaffold
This repository contains a minimal scaffold -- frontend, backend, and database wired together, but no whiteboard logic. You are free to restructure anything in the scaffold, but you are expected to stay within the provided stack; don't swap out the framework or database.

How we evaluate
We evaluate whether your submission has a functional implementation of all required features above and is fit for the deployment context. Correctness and robustness for this use case and set of features matters more to us than other features beyond the required list, such as those listed as out of scope..

Use of AI
Use whatever AI tools you like. We recommend that you use frontier AI coding models. We are not aware of a candidate who has performed exceptionally on this take-home without using AI. However, note that you will be expected to fully understand and defend your implementation during the call after the solo block.

Yes, but use it carefully.

This repo is a good fit for the skill because the hard part is not scaffolding, itтАЩs freezing the shared contract for:
- shape schema
- whiteboard operation model
- websocket message types
- cursor/presence events
- undo/redo semantics
- persistence boundaries
- auth/invite surface

That is exactly what the skill is for.

**Where it helps most**
Use the skill at the start to produce a contract pack, not to immediately unleash a big write swarm.

For this take-home, the riskiest drift is:
- client and server disagreeing on shape/event payloads
- undo/redo semantics not matching persistence
- presence/cursor events being modeled separately from board operations
- тАЬsame object edited by multiple usersтАЭ having undefined conflict behavior

So the skill can give you a clean first 15-20 minute architecture pass.

**Big constraint for this repo**
The assignment says:
- work on `main`
- commit early and often
- push directly to `main`

That means I would **not** use a fancy branch/worktree-heavy swarm workflow here.
I would use the skill in a lighter mode:
- contract-first planning
- maybe one builder and one validator

whats the estimated build time for this repo

For this repo as it exists now, with the scaffold basically empty:

**Realistic estimate**
- `2.5 to 3 hours`: functional but narrow, if you stay very simple and use AI well
- `4 to 6 hours`: solid and defensible implementation of all required features
- `6+ hours`: robust, cleaned up, well-tested version

So for the actual Mechanize take-home, the honest answer is:

**Yes, it is possible in 3 hours, but only if you build a ruthless MVP.**

**Why**
The scaffold gives you almost nothing beyond:
- React shell
- FastAPI shell
- Postgres connection
- empty websocket loop

The expensive parts are:
- whiteboard object model
- real-time sync
- undo/redo
- auth + invites
- persistence
- client/server protocol alignment

ThatтАЩs a lot for a near-empty repo.

**My repo-specific breakdown**
- `15-20 min`: contract and architecture freeze
- `20-30 min`: schema + backend scaffolding + auth basics
- `45-60 min`: realtime board protocol + persistence
- `45-60 min`: frontend canvas/editor interactions
- `20-30 min`: invites + canvas open/create flow
- `20-30 min`: integration, bug fixing, validation

That lands around `3 to 4 hours` for a credible pass.

**What makes it fit in 3**
You need to keep these aggressively simple:
- auth: basic session/JWT flow, no polish
- invites: simplest acceptable mechanism
- conflict handling: last-write-wins
- persistence: store canonical board state, donтАЩt build elaborate versioning
- undo/redo: keep it simple and predictable, not perfect under all concurrency edge cases
- canvas: fixed viewport, no pan/zoom, no fancy UX

**What will blow the schedule**
- overengineering websocket protocol
- fancy state architecture on the frontend
- trying to make undo/redo perfect under simultaneous conflicting edits
- polishing UI
- adding unnecessary abstractions early

**My blunt estimate**
If your goal is тАЬall required features, defendable in interview,тАЭ treat this as a `3.5 hour job compressed into 3`.
If your goal is тАЬship something that passes the bar inside the window,тАЭ itтАЩs doable.

# Review findings:

## Finding 1 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//server//app//ws.py:202-226) [added]
[P2] Malformed websocket canvas IDs are not rejected cleanly

The pre-accept membership query passes the raw `canvasId` string into a UUID-backed query without validating it first. An invalid canvas id can raise an asyncpg conversion error before the socket is accepted, turning a bad URL into an internal error instead of a clean auth or membership rejection.

## Finding 2 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:615-624) [added]
[P0] Client-side optimistic reconciliation ignores server ordering

The client applies its own op immediately, then later drops the echoed op if the `opId` matches. If another user's conflicting op arrives before that echo, this client preserves local-first ordering instead of the server's `seq` ordering and can diverge from the authoritative board state. Same-field concurrent edits are especially vulnerable here. The client needs seq-aware reconciliation or a pending-op rebase, not a blind 'skip own echo' rule.

## Finding 3 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:121-135) [added]
[P1] Local edits are silently dropped while the socket is not open

`sendOp` mutates local state and records the op as pending before attempting to send it, but `sendWs` becomes a no-op unless the socket is already `OPEN`. Any draw, move, resize, delete, or recolor performed during startup or after a disconnect appears locally but never reaches the server, so it disappears on refresh and never reaches collaborators.

## Finding 4 (C://Users//sahil//OneDrive//Documents//GitHub//Mechanize_takehome//client//src//components//CanvasPage.tsx:573-578) [added]
[P1] Initial HTTP load can overwrite newer websocket state

The canvas detail fetch and the websocket session run in parallel, and both overwrite `shapesRef.current`. If the GET resolves after `init` or after one or more live `op` messages, the older HTTP snapshot can clobber newer real-time state on this client. Gate the HTTP result so it only seeds state before websocket init, or make websocket init the sole source of initial board state.

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

# Mechanize Take-Home: Collaborative Whiteboard

Thank you for taking the time to interview with Mechanize! This repo contains your assignment for the final phase of our interview process, an open-ended take-home assignment.

The take-home begins with a solo work block of three hours in which you can complete the task as described below.

There is a 20-minute break after the solo block. During this time, our team will review your submission. If we'd like to move forward based on your progress, we'll talk to you on the follow-up call. If not, we'll cancel the follow-up calendar invite and send you an email cancelling the call.


## Getting started

1. **Clone this repo** (do _not_ fork it):
   ```bash
   git clone <this-repo-url>
   cd <this-repo-name>
   ```
2. See `SETUP.md` for how to run the scaffold.
3. Do all of your work on the **`main` branch**. Commit early and often.
4. **Push your commits directly to `main`** ├втВмтАЭ do not open a pull request.
   ```bash
   git push origin main
   ```
5. Your **final commit must be pushed no later than three hours** after your start time.
   Anything pushed after the deadline will not be considered.

## What you're building

A real-time collaborative whiteboard. Multiple users, same canvas, editing together.

## Deployment context

This whiteboard is for synchronous design reviews, consisting of a small group (typically 2-5 people) on a call together, using the canvas as a shared scratchpad while they talk through a design. 
- Each user has an account and can create canvases or be invited to others'. 
- It's common for several people to be editing at once -- often the same objects -- as they try alternatives or work through a disagreement in real time. 
- A typical session runs 30-90 minutes. 
- Teams sometimes return to a canvas in a later session to recall what was decided.

## Required features

- User accounts (signup, login, logout)
- Create a canvas, open an existing canvas
- Invite other users to a canvas (by username or email -- a simple mechanism is fine)
- Shapes: rectangle, ellipse, line, text
- Select, move, resize, delete, change color
- Undo / redo
- See other users' cursors
- Canvas state persists across sessions

## Out of scope

Don't spend time on these feature, as we won't evaluate them:

- Collaborative text editing within text elements (treating a text update as a single operation is fine -- you don't need to handle two users typing in the same text box simultaneously)
- Granular permissions (viewer/editor/owner roles, share-with-link, etc.)
- Rich text editing, images, or file uploads
- Canvas zoom, pan, or infinite canvas
- Export (PNG, SVG, PDF)
- Version history or named snapshots
- Mobile or touch support

## Provided scaffold

This repository contains a minimal scaffold -- frontend, backend, and database wired together, but no whiteboard logic. You are free to restructure anything in the scaffold, but you are expected to stay within the provided stack; don't swap out the framework or database.

## How we evaluate

We evaluate whether your submission has a functional implementation of all required features above and is fit for the deployment context. Correctness and robustness for this use case and set of features matters more to us than other features beyond the required list, such as those listed as out of scope..

## Use of AI

Use whatever AI tools you like. We recommend that you use frontier AI coding models. We are not aware of a candidate who has performed exceptionally on this take-home without using AI. However, note that you will be expected to **fully understand and defend** your implementation during the call after the solo block.

 173:     shapes = []

 145: # ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм Ping/Pong heartbeat ├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм├втАЭтВм
 146: 
 147: PING_INTERVAL = 30  # seconds between pings
 148: PONG_TIMEOUT = 10   # seconds to wait for pong reply
 149: 
 150: 
 151: async def _heartbeat_loop(
 152:     ws: WebSocket,
 153:     send_lock: asyncio.Lock,
 154:     pong_event: asyncio.Event,
 155:     cancel_event: asyncio.Event,
 156: ) -> None:
 157:     """Send periodic pings and close the connection if pong is not received in time."""
 158:     try:
 159:         while not cancel_event.is_set():
 160:             # Wait PING_INTERVAL seconds or until cancelled
 161:             try:
 162:                 await asyncio.wait_for(cancel_event.wait(), timeout=PING_INTERVAL)
 163:                 return  # cancel_event was set
 164:             except asyncio.TimeoutError:
 165:                 pass  # time to ping
 166: 
 167:             # Send ping
 168:             pong_event.clear()
 169:             await _safe_send(ws, json.dumps({"type": "ping"}), send_lock)
 170: 
 171:             # Wait for pong
 172:             try:
 173:                 await asyncio.wait_for(pong_event.wait(), timeout=PONG_TIMEOUT)
 174:             except asyncio.TimeoutError:
 175:                 # No pong received ├втВмтАЭ close the connection
 176:                 try:
 177:                     await ws.close(code=1000, reason="pong timeout")
 178:                 except Exception:
 179:                     pass
 180:                 return
 181:     except asyncio.CancelledError:
 182:         pass
